[In recent years there have been some great resources on metrics for startups, in particular Dave McClures metrics for pirates and Andrew Chens articles on user acquisition and KissMetrics article on conversion. Over the last few products I worked on I synthesized this into a core model that I found very helpful. Here are some questions I had difficulty understanding early on that led to my approach:
\* You notice that your power users all have taken some action (e.g. filled out their profile) so you try to encourage all users to fill out their profile to get them more hooked on your product. Does this actually help?
\* You have 24 hours of downtime, the next day you come back up your traffic is down. Will this have a long-term effect you need to worry about?
\* You have 100K uniques per day and so does your competitor, but are these 100K people who come back every day or 700K people who each come once per week? Does it matter?
\* You turn on a new advertising campaign and see your # of unique visitors per day start to increase, you assume that this will continue increasing so long as you keep the ads running, right?
\* You start having email deliverability problems (or Facebook turns off notifications) so you can’t notify users of new activity on the site. The # of unique visitors decreases slightly but you’re not too worried, should you be?
What follows is a detailed how-to for analyzing your product’s customers.
The key insight is this: the most important thing is how many unique customers you have, and by implication, knowing what causes any change in this number. And by customers I don’t mean visitors or pageviews, I mean the subset of those people that will be long-term repeat users. Knowing how to measure those customers is the key concept that connects your pageviews, visitors, conversion funnel, retention %s, and all those other numbers you’re collecting and not quite sure how to make sense of. This isthe heart of your products and business’s success.
Here is the simple model:

The first step is to figure out how many new customers you add each day and to do this you have to define what makes a customer.
People seeing your product for the first time go through a series of first-time-visitor steps and then start using your product (landing page, registration, inviting their friends, etc). People usually refer to this series of steps as their new user funnel and the end of the funnel is usually some step like uploading their first photo or completing the first level in your game—some core action for your product. But where should your funnel end? This is actually a critical question that a lot of people get wrong; they misdefine the point of transition from a new visitor to an actual customer.
Here is how to figure it out. First, hopefully you’re already measuring the conversion from each step in the funnel to the next step: 50% of people who see the landing page click the sign up link, 60% of people who start signing up complete the process, etc. Ask yourself, when someone has been using your product for awhile, what count as a meaningful return visit? Say you have a photo sharing website. If someone returns but only looks at one page and doesn’t click anything, that probably doesn’t count. But if they come and get sucked in and view 5+ unique photos, I’d say that counts. Or if they view one photo but they comment on it, that probably counts too. Once you’ve defined a meaningful visit then you need to work backwards and find the point where % chance of a repeat meaningful visit is 95+ %.
To switch examples, say you created an online game. After your user completes the sign-up process and takes their first few turns, there is still a good chance they will not return. However, those users who have come back on 100 unique days (where they took a turn on that day) should have a near 100% chance of returning the 101st time to take a turn. Find the point in between where the drop-off hits 5%, meaning 95% of people who reach this point will return. At this point you can call someone a customer. In a game I worked on once, after someone cast 24 spells and had visited the game on at least two unique days, they were hooked. We only lost 1% of these people each day. This point was the end of our new user funnel.
(Note: if you don’t have a very sticky point like this, you may not have created a high-value product yet)
The second step is to figure out when you’ve lost a customer. After you’ve converted them from a visitor to a customer, by definition, you know you have a high likelihood of them engaging with the product again, but even these customers may not visit every single day. At what point have you lost them?
You should have an intuition about the interval between meaningful visits that you care about; start there and look for an unexpectedly long gap. In the game I mentioned above most people played every day (it was designed with this intent). A month after launch I grabbed all my customers who had played today and had been around for at least three weeks. I looked at how long of a break these people took. Lots of people skipped a day of playing, many skipped two or three consecutive days of playing, but very few took people ever took a 4 day break and came back again so after 4 days I considered a customer to be dead. For a second example, consider a product like Shazam on the iPhone (it identifies a song you are listening to). I use this product once month. Assuming this is okay to Shazam as a company (meaning that they can still build a successful business on people like me) then their lost point will be much different than with my game. I bet some Shazam users skip a month or two, but if you’ve skimmed four months you’ve probably forgotten about Shazam and have a very low chance of returning.
Carrying Capacity
At this point, every day you know how many customers you gained, how many you lost, and how many total you currently have. With this data you can now calculate your carrying capacity.
Lets say you have 400 new visitors each day (i.e. from Adwords or Facebook Ads) and 50 of them turn into new customers; and of your current customers you lose lose 1% of them each day. Your product will continue to grow until you reach 5000 customers and then it will stop. Why? Because the # of customers you add each day is mostly independent of your total audience size, whereas the # of customers you lose each day will steadily increase until you’re losing the exact same number of customers you gain each day. At this point you plateau.
Once you have reached this equilibrium state, if you increase your daily ad spend so you’re acquiring 75 new customers each day you’ll start growing again until you reach 7,500 total customers then you’ll stop. And conversely, if you keep your # of new customers the same but figure out how to get your daily loss to 0.66% you will also grow to 7500 customers and then level off.
Carrying Capacity = # of new daily customers / % customers you lost each day
(The reality is a little more complex than this because the # of new customers you get each day is influenced to some extent by the total # of customers you have, but you can tease out those effects later.)
Enhancing the Model
The next thing to understand is where your new customers are coming from. There are 3 possibilities worth isolating:
\* This is a brand new person you’ve never seen before (usually from advertising, SEO, or referral)
\* This is a former customer you considered lost or dead that has been resurrected
\* This is someone who tried your product in the past but never became a customer; now they’ve come back and converted (I call these the skeptics)
Here is the original diagram expanded. In this picture you’ve added 50 new customers today but you isolated the three different sources (new, skeptics saved, resurrected). You also track the intermediate pools (skeptics and dead customers).
￼
There is one more expansion I find helpful. Within the 5,000 total customers pictured above, some % of those returned today so they’re as good as new. Here is what my full customer dashboard with that addition:
￼
Virality
Virality is the one concept which has the biggest potential to impact this model. Most people look at virality in a simplified way. You take all the new people who tried your product today and see how many friends they brought (either today or on any day in the near future). If 50 people came today and they inspired 25 of friends to come over the next few days then you’re K-factor is 0.5 (each new visitor will bring 0.5 friends). This doesn’t have any impact on the model I explained above. See the 400 new visitors? If your K-factor is 0.5 this just means you only had to buy 266 of them, the other 134 came for free. Correction: You only had to buy 200 of them, the other 200 came for free. I was only considering the second generation of free visitors, but these will bring a third generation, which will bring a fourth, etc. A K-factor of 0.5 means 1/(1–0.5)=2 times the number of users you bought will ultimately come. Any K-factor less than 1.0 is essentially just a discount on advertising spend (0.5 equals a 33% discount 50% discount).
What most people overlook is that retention has a huge effect on your K-factor. Sure, most of the invitations people send occur in the first visit or first few days of using a product; this is why you initially spend so much time optimizing your new user funnel and trying to get people to refer their friends as part of this. However, after this first-visit kicker most users who come to love your product and stay around for a long time will continue to refer a steady stream of their friends. This can give you a nice bump to your lifetime K-factor and give you a greater chance of getting it over 1.0.
Note: In the case that your first-visit viral coefficient is greater than 1.0, things can be very confusing without having a clear model like the one above. You could be converting 0% of your visitors to customers (i.e. all the water going into your bucket is instantly flowing out), but you’re steadily turning up the water since each new visitor is bringing more than one friend. You will see a steady increase in all your naive measurements of success (i.e. unique pageviews and visitors per day) but in reality you may not be acquiring any customers.
Benefit
In closing, lets revisit the example questions I raised at the start. Here is how this model helps:
\* You notice that your power users all have taken some action (e.g. filled out their profile) so you try to encourage all users to fill out their profile to get them more hooked on your product. Does this actually help? Without this model its easy to mislead yourself into thinking it helps. You will probably be able to increase the metric but it may just weaken the correlation with power users. However, with this model you just watch your loss % each day and if it doesn’t change then you haven’t had any net impact on retention.
\* You have 24 hours of downtime, the next day you come back up your traffic is down. Will this have a long-term effect you need to worry about? Even if your traffic is lowered for a few days, all you have to do is measure your new visitors per day & lost customers per day and you’ll see if your carrying capacity has changed. If it has not changed then you don’t have to worry, you know your traffic will rise back up to your carrying capacity.
\* You have 100K uniques per day and so does your competitor, but are these 100K people who come back every day or 700K people who each come once per week? Does it matter? If you’re incorrectly caught up in # of unique visitors per day then this does seem like an important thing. And in fact, if you realize your visitors are not returning as often as your competitors you may even be tempted to spam them regularly because this will increase # of unique visitors each day and can give you the illusion of improving things. In reality a move like this would probably increase your % lost each day and hurt your carrying capacity but you wouldn’t notice this for awhile because the increased # of uniques each day would mask the harm. However, with the model your emphasis is on # of customers not # of visitors. You’ll quickly realize that you don’t care how frequently people visit as a primary factor; if it doesn’t impact # of new customers per day or % of lost per day then you haven’t actually helped your product.
\* You turn on a new advertising campaign and see your # of unique visitors per day start to increase, you assume that this will continue increasing so long as you keep the ads running, right? Nope, you’ll level off once you’ve reached your new carrying capacity.
\* You start having email deliverability problems (or Facebook turns off notifications) so you cant notify users of new activity on the site. The # of unique visitors per day decreases slightly but you’re not too worried, should you be? This is similar to question 3, it may or may not be important but you’ll quickly be able to tell by focusing on the core numbers. Increasing # of unique visitors per day does not necessarily lead to more total customers.]

Upgradation of rover to a containerized distributed and scalable solution which can run on fargate containers came with a lot of challenges and required in-depth knowledge in distributing and scaling AI solutions. 
Some of the important upgrades associated with the same is listed below. 
Containerizing rover in such a way that it can work without GPU required rewriting and optimizing code to work with CPU without loosing performance. 
Redesigned and implemented the distributed architecture in a loosely coupled fashion with multiple containers so that more worker containers can be spun up when there are more requests.
Changed workers to run in a preforked fashion so that we can utilise the multiprocessing capabilities effectively and run more than one exploration in a single container. 
Implemented service discovery among containers so that they can communicate with each other for better task handling. 
Solved connection pool issues while explorations are updating files in s3 containers. 
Improved file handling with pathlib modules to reduce redundancy and improve reliability. 
Profiled CPU and Memory and Refactored code in such a way that it will have lower cpu and memory footprint. Each worker container can work with 512 MB ram and 0.5 vCPUs. 
Integration with aws managed redis queue and fixed issues associated with worker not able to access the tasks. 
Adapted better and more secure task serialisation 
Added dynamic port allocation for workers and added it as metadata in the Asynchronous Task results. 
Added key based authentication for connecting devices to rover containers. 
Better task abort with revoke using socket connection and remote procedure calls. 
Improved observability and monitoring of queues and task status using flower which is deployed in a separate container.

Apologies for the oversight. Here's an updated example with the filtering by ID located in the email body.

First, let's modify the `email_producer.py` script to extract the email ID from the email body and use it as the partition key:
```python
import re
import json

async def get_email_id(message):
    # Extract the email ID from the email body
    text_part = message.text_part.get_payload().decode(message.text_part.charset)
    match = re.search(r'ID:\s*(\w+)', text_part)
    return match.group(1) if match else None

async def on_new_email(producer, uid, mail):
    raw_message = await mail.fetch([uid], ['BODY[]', 'FLAGS'])
    message = pyzmail36.PyzMessage.factory(raw_message[uid][b'BODY[]'])
    email_id = await get_email_id(message)

    if email_id:
        subject = message.get_subject()
        from_email = message.get_addresses('from')

        email_data = {
            'uid': uid,
            'subject': subject,
            'from': from_email
        }

        # Send the email data to the Kafka topic using the email ID as the partition key
        await producer.send_and_wait("email_topic", value=json.dumps(email_data).encode(), key=email_id.encode())

# The rest of the email_producer.py remains the same
```
Now, create a custom consumer group for each `email_consumer.py` instance and filter the consumed messages based on the ID:
```python
import json

# Define the email ID that this instance should process
instance_email_id = 'some_id'

async def consume_messages(consumer, mail):
    async for msg in consumer:
        # Get the email ID from the message key
        email_id = msg.key.decode()

        # Process the email only if the email ID matches the instance_email_id
        if email_id == instance_email_id:
            uid = int(json.loads(msg.value)['uid'])
            await process_email(mail, uid)

async def main(email, app_password, kafka_bootstrap_servers):
    imap_server = 'outlook.office365.com'
    mail = aioimaplib.IMAP4_SSL(host=imap_server, timeout=10)
    await mail.wait_hello_from_server()

    await mail.login(email, app_password)
    await mail.select('INBOX')

    consumer = AIOKafkaConsumer(
        "email_topic",
        bootstrap_servers=kafka_bootstrap_servers,
        group_id=f"email_consumer_group_{instance_email_id}",
        value_deserializer=lambda m: m.decode("utf-8"),
        key_deserializer=lambda m: m.decode("utf-8")
    )
    await consumer.start()

    try:
        await consume_messages(consumer, mail)
    finally:
        await consumer.stop()

# The rest of the email_consumer.py remains the same
```
With this setup, each instance of `email_consumer.py` will only process emails with the specified email ID in the email body. You can run multiple instances with different values for `instance_email_id` to handle different subsets of emails. Note that the example assumes that the email body contains a line with the format `ID: some_id`. You should modify the `get_email_id` function in the `email_producer.py` script to match the actual format of the email ID in the email body.

Yes, Lua code can emit Apache Arrow record batches as output, without the need to write to a file.

To do this, you can use the `arrow.RecordBatchBuilder` class from either the `mdavezac/arrow-lua` or `luapower/arrow` libraries. This class allows you to build an Arrow record batch incrementally, by adding columns and rows of data.

Here's an example of how to use `arrow.RecordBatchBuilder` to build an Arrow record batch in Lua:
```lua
local arrow = require("arrow")
local builder = arrow.RecordBatchBuilder:new()
builder:add_field("id", arrow.int32())
builder:add_field("name", arrow.utf8())
builder:start()
builder:append(1, "Alice")
builder:append(2, "Bob")
builder:append(3, "Charlie")
local record_batch = builder:finish()
-- Use the record_batch here
```
In this example, we use the `require` function to import the `arrow` module from either the `mdavezac/arrow-lua` or `luapower/arrow` library. We then create a `RecordBatchBuilder` instance and add two fields to it: an integer field called "id" and a UTF-8 string field called "name". We start the record batch and add three rows of data using the `append` method. Finally, we finish building the record batch and store it in a variable called `record_batch`.

Note that the exact API and syntax may vary between different Arrow libraries for Lua. Be sure to check the documentation for the specific library you're using.

Include the following key points:
A few weeks ago, a Digital Executive at a large Swiss corporate asked me if Ginetta could also support on their Digital Business Strategy. I said “It depends. If you are looking for a 300-page strategy paper, then probably not. However, if you are looking to create a Digital Business Strategy that is truly customer-centered, then maybe yes”.
My point was that we are not a business consulting firm that creates 5-year transformation programs, with the hope that change will come out at the other end of the tunnel.
We are a Digital Experience Company. And part of that is Strategy too. However, everything we do needs to have a clear purpose with the human in the center.
So often, companies approach change management top down. They hire consultants to map out change programs and transformation initiatives. And they often look very good on paper. However, they are also often hard to put into practice because they are detached from the human.
Making change is hard. People, by design, don’t want to change. Sure, some do. But most don’t.
However, change is almost impossible if you don’t put the human in the center.

I'm doing research for why beginner's struggle with starting and growing their Amazon FBA business to 6-7 figures in 2023.

I realized that the problem is all of them are trying to find that one big product with the best profit margins, which sells like crazy. But growing an Amazon FBA business to 7 figures doesn't work that way

Instead of trying to find 1 big product, you implement the "12 month phase plan"

The 12 month phase plan has 3 phases:
Phase 1: 0-$10k

Your goal is to get some initial profit. You’ll try to get products which are trending, have relatively high profit margins, need not be scalable or consistent. 

The goal is to just make your first $10k in this phase.

Phase 2: $10k-100k

In this phase, you have to find a more scalable and consistent product. Your goal here is not to make some quick cash. But instead, it’s to find a consistent sell product, with good margins that can make you your first $100k.

Phase 3: $100k+

You either build a portfolio of products from phase 2 

or

You switch to private label and scale to 7 figures.

I want you to do two things:

1. Act as a 7 figure Amazon FBA expert with years of experience and explain what I just explained you in an easy to understand, informal, personal and casual tone. Tell me that you understand my struggles, list out my struggles and pains. Next, talk about how trying to find 1 big product to scale to 7 figures doesn't work. Next explain the "12 month phase plan" and how it has the potential to solve all of my problems

2. Brainstorm and generate 20 catchy nicknames I can use for the "12 month phase plan"

One day, Lucy's sister Emily was tidying up the living room when she accidentally sucked Lucy up with the vacuum cleaner. Emily had been moving the vacuum around the room, trying to get all of the dust and dirt off the floor, when she suddenly felt the vacuum start to pull harder than usual.

She looked down and saw that the vacuum hose had sucked up Lucy, who was lying on the floor at the time. Emily immediately panicked and turned off the vacuum, fearing that she had injured her sister.

When she pulled the hose out, she saw that Lucy was flattened even more than usual, and she looked quite scared and upset. Emily immediately apologized and helped Lucy sit up, trying to comfort her and make sure that she was okay.

Despite her initial fright, Lucy was relieved to see that she was unharmed. She laughed and told Emily not to worry, saying that she had always been a bit of a troublemaker and that this was just one more adventure to add to her list.

Feeling relieved and grateful that her sister was okay, Emily helped Lucy back to her usual spot on the floor and promised to be more careful in the future. From then on, Emily made sure to keep a close eye on Lucy whenever she was using the vacuum, making sure that she didn't accidentally suck her up again.

Despite the occasional mishap, the sisters continued to have fun and enjoy each other's company, always looking out for one another and supporting each other through thick and thin.

import numpy as np
import imageio
import os
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.interpolate import Rbf
from scipy.interpolate import interp1d
import scipy.ndimage as ndimage
from scipy.stats import norm

def get\_images(image\_folder):
 images = []
 for image\_name in os.listdir(image\_folder):
 image\_path = os.path.join(image\_folder, image\_name)
 with open(image\_path, 'rb') as f:
 images.append(imageio.imread(f))
 return images

def get\_average\_rgb(image\_path):
 image = imageio.imread(image\_path)
 average\_rgb = np.mean(image, axis=(0, 1)) / 255
 return average\_rgb

def get\_rgb\_coordinates(image\_folder):
 rgb\_coordinates = []
 for image\_name in os.listdir(image\_folder):
 image\_path = os.path.join(image\_folder, image\_name)
 average\_rgb = get\_average\_rgb(image\_path)
 rgb\_coordinates.append(average\_rgb)
 return np.array(rgb\_coordinates)

def get\_curve\_input\_output(source\_channel, target\_channel):
 source\_histogram, \_ = np.histogram(source\_channel, bins=255, range=(0, 255), density=True)
 target\_histogram, \_ = np.histogram(target\_channel, bins=255, range=(0, 255), density=True)
 cumulative\_source\_histogram = np.cumsum(source\_histogram)
 cumulative\_target\_histogram = np.cumsum(target\_histogram)
 input\_values = cumulative\_source\_histogram \* 255
 output\_values = cumulative\_target\_histogram \* 255
 return input\_values, output\_values

def create\_curve(input\_values, output\_values):
 curve = interp1d(input\_values, output\_values, bounds\_error=False, fill\_value=(output\_values[0], output\_values[-1]))
 return curve

def apply\_r\_curve\_transformation(source\_grayscale, target\_grayscale):
 source\_images = get\_images(source\_grayscale)
 target\_images = get\_images(target\_grayscale)

 for source\_image, target\_image in zip(source\_images, target\_images):
 source\_r\_channel = source\_image[..., 0]
 target\_r\_channel = target\_image[..., 0]
 input\_values, output\_values = get\_curve\_input\_output(source\_r\_channel, target\_r\_channel)
 r\_curve = create\_curve(input\_values, output\_values)
 source\_image[..., 0] = r\_curve(source\_r\_channel)

def apply\_g\_curve\_transformation(source\_grayscale, target\_grayscale):
 source\_images = get\_images(source\_grayscale)
 target\_images = get\_images(target\_grayscale)

 for source\_image, target\_image in zip(source\_images, target\_images):
 source\_g\_channel = source\_image[..., 1]
 target\_g\_channel = target\_image[..., 1]
 input\_values, output\_values = get\_curve\_input\_output(source\_g\_channel, target\_g\_channel)
 g\_curve = create\_curve(input\_values, output\_values)
 source\_image[..., 1] = g\_curve(source\_g\_channel)

def apply\_b\_curve\_transformation(source\_grayscale, target\_grayscale):
 source\_images = get\_images(source\_grayscale)
 target\_images = get\_images(target\_grayscale)

 for source\_image, target\_image in zip(source\_images, target\_images):
 source\_b\_channel = source\_image[..., 2]
 target\_b\_channel = target\_image[..., 2]
 input\_values, output\_values = get\_curve\_input\_output(source\_b\_channel, target\_b\_channel)
 b\_curve = create\_curve(input\_values, output\_values)
 source\_image[..., 2] = b\_curve(source\_b\_channel)

def generate\_3d\_lut(source\_folder, source\_grayscale, target\_folder, target\_grayscale, lut\_size, epsilon=1e-5, sigma=1, smoothing\_on=False):
 source\_rgb\_coordinates = get\_rgb\_coordinates(source\_folder)
 source\_gray\_coordinates = get\_rgb\_coordinates(source\_grayscale)
 target\_rgb\_coordinates = get\_rgb\_coordinates(target\_folder)
 target\_gray\_coordinates = get\_rgb\_coordinates(target\_grayscale)
 source\_coordinates = np.concatenate((source\_rgb\_coordinates, source\_gray\_coordinates), axis=0)
 target\_coordinates = np.concatenate((target\_rgb\_coordinates, target\_gray\_coordinates), axis=0)

 # Perform 1st order transformation
 target\_coordinates[:, 0] = apply\_r\_curve\_transformation(source\_coordinates[:, 0], target\_coordinates[:, 0])
 target\_coordinates[:, 1] = apply\_g\_curve\_transformation(source\_coordinates[:, 1], target\_coordinates[:, 1])
 target\_coordinates[:, 2] = apply\_b\_curve\_transformation(source\_coordinates[:, 2], target\_coordinates[:, 2])

 # Create a 100x100x100 3D grid
 x = np.linspace(0, 1, lut\_size)
 y = np.linspace(0, 1, lut\_size)
 z = np.linspace(0, 1, lut\_size)
 grid\_x, grid\_y, grid\_z = np.meshgrid(x, y, z)

 # Flatten the grid for interpolation
 grid\_x = grid\_x.flatten()
 grid\_y = grid\_y.flatten()
 grid\_z = grid\_z.flatten()

 # Use the Rbf method for interpolation for red, green, and blue axis
 rbf\_r = Rbf(source\_coordinates[:, 0], source\_coordinates[:, 1], source\_coordinates[:, 2], target\_coordinates[:, 0], epsilon=epsilon)
 rbf\_g = Rbf(source\_coordinates[:, 0], source\_coordinates[:, 1], source\_coordinates[:, 2], target\_coordinates[:, 1], epsilon=epsilon)
 rbf\_b = Rbf(source\_coordinates[:, 0], source\_coordinates[:, 1], source\_coordinates[:, 2], target\_coordinates[:, 2], epsilon=epsilon)
 target\_rgb\_values = np.array([rbf\_r(grid\_z, grid\_x, grid\_y), rbf\_g(grid\_z, grid\_x, grid\_y), rbf\_b(grid\_z, grid\_x, grid\_y)]).T

 #Guassian Smoothing
 if smoothing\_on:
 target\_rgb\_values = ndimage.gaussian\_filter(target\_rgb\_values, sigma)

 # Write LUT to file
 with open(file\_path, "w") as f:
 f.write(f"# LUT size\nLUT\_3D\_SIZE {lut\_size}\n\n")
 for i, target\_rgb in enumerate(target\_rgb\_values):
 f.write(f"{target\_rgb[0]:.6f} {target\_rgb[1]:.6f} {target\_rgb[2]:.6f}\n")

 #Plot the 3D LUT transformation
 fig = plt.figure()
 ax = fig.add\_subplot(111, projection='3d')
 ax.scatter(target\_rgb\_values[:, 0], target\_rgb\_values[:, 1], target\_rgb\_values[:, 2], c='r', marker='o')
 plt.show()

```python
import sys
from vyattaconfparser import parse_conf

# Format string for hosts file entry
hosts_entry_format = "{ip_address}\t{hostname}\t#{mac_address}\n"

# Define the folder containing the EdgeOS configuration file
config_folder = "/nfs/home/config/edgemax"

# Find the newest EdgeOS configuration file in the config folder
config_file = max(
    [os.path.join(config_folder, f) for f in os.listdir(config_folder) if f.startswith("config.boot")],
    key=os.path.getctime,
)

# Parse the EdgeOS configuration file into a dictionary
with open(config_file, "r") as f:
    config = parse_conf(f.read())

# Get the DHCP static mappings from the configuration dictionary
shared_networks = config.get("service", {}).get("dhcp-server", {}).get("shared-network-name", {})

# If there are no static mappings, exit with a non-zero code
if not shared_networks:
    sys.exit(1)

# Generate a hosts file from the static mappings
hosts_entries = []
for shared_network in shared_networks.values():
    for subnet in shared_network.get("subnet", {}).values():
        for hostname, static_mapping in subnet.get("static-mapping", {}).items():
            mac_address = static_mapping.get("mac-address", "")
            ip_address = static_mapping.get("ip-address", "")
            if all([ip_address, hostname]):
                hosts_entry = hosts_entry_format.format(
                    ip_address=ip_address,
                    hostname=hostname,
                    mac_address=mac_address,
                )
                hosts_entries.append(hosts_entry)

# Write the hosts file to stdout or to a file if the result is not empty
if hosts_entries:
    sys.stdout.writelines(hosts_entries)
else:
    sys.exit(1)
```
This updated script correctly extracts the DHCP static mappings from the EdgeOS configuration file and generates a hosts file from them.

Certainly! Here are 10 examples of discretionary trading strategies:

1. Trend following with filters: This strategy involves using various filters, such as moving averages or relative strength index, to identify trending assets and then taking positions in those assets based on the direction of the trend.
2. Contrarian trading: This strategy involves taking positions opposite to the majority of market participants, on the assumption that the crowd is often wrong.
3. Sector rotation: This strategy involves switching between different sectors of the market based on their relative strength or weakness.
4. Event-driven trading: This strategy involves taking positions based on specific events, such as earnings announcements or regulatory changes.
5. News-based trading: This strategy involves taking positions based on news and other publicly available information.
6. Sentiment analysis: This strategy involves taking positions based on the sentiment of market participants, as reflected in social media or other sources of data.
7. Technical analysis: This strategy involves taking positions based on chart patterns and other technical indicators, such as support and resistance levels.
8. Fundamental analysis: This strategy involves taking positions based on the underlying financial and economic conditions of the assets being traded, such as earnings and growth potential.
9. Breakout trading: This strategy involves taking positions when the price breaks above a resistance level or below a support level, with the assumption that the breakout is a significant event.
10. Momentum trading: This strategy involves taking positions in assets with high momentum, on the assumption that the momentum will continue.

Coupa, Athena, and SAP HANA are all different types of software that have different uses and capabilities.

1. Coupa is a cloud-based software platform that provides businesses with a suite of financial management tools, specifically designed to help businesses manage their spending, from procurement to invoicing and payments.
2. Athena is a program developed by Amazon Web Services (AWS) that allows users to run SQL-like queries on data stored in Amazon S3, a cloud-based object storage service. It is used for data lake, data warehousing and big data analytics, business intelligence, and machine learning scenarios.
3. SAP HANA is an in-memory data platform that is used to process high-volume, real-time data. It provides advanced analytics, data processing, and application development capabilities.

A Project Lead is a person who is responsible for managing and coordinating the implementation of a project. In this context, a Project Lead for the implementation of Coupa, Athena, and SAP HANA would be responsible for managing the process of implementing these systems within an organization. The project lead would be responsible for ensuring that the project is completed on time, within budget, and to the satisfaction of all stakeholders. The Project Lead would be the main point of contact between the organization and the vendors, and would be responsible for managing the project team, which can include internal staff and external consultants.

The Project Lead would need to have a good understanding of the organization's business processes, as well as the capabilities and limitations of the software being implemented. They should have experience managing projects and leading teams, as well as strong communication, problem-solving, and leadership skills.

As I approached my 25th birthday, I found myself feeling restless and unfulfilled. Despite my love for music and my early success as an artist, I couldn't shake the feeling that something was missing in my life. I had fallen into a routine, working a job that paid the bills but didn't fulfill me creatively or emotionally. I was comfortable, but not truly happy.

As the day of my birthday approached, I began to reflect on my life and my dreams. I thought about all of the musical opportunities I had let slip by, all of the songs I had never written, all of the moments I had missed because I was too scared to take a risk. I realized that I had reached a crossroads in my life, and I had to make a choice. I could continue down my current path, living a comfortable but unfulfilling life, or I could take a risk and pursue my dreams, no matter what the cost.

It was a difficult decision, but as I blew out the candles on my birthday cake, I knew what I had to do. I was done living a life that didn't fulfill me. I was done letting my fears hold me back. I was ready to take a risk and pursue my dreams, no matter what it took.

The first step in my journey was facing my fears head-on. I was scared of the unknown, of failing, of not being good enough. But I knew that I couldn't let those fears hold me back any longer. I took a deep breath and stepped out of my comfort zone, reaching out to musicians and industry professionals who could help me take the next step in my career.

One of the musicians I reached out to was a seasoned veteran in the industry, someone I had looked up to for years. I was nervous to speak with him, but I knew that if I didn't take this opportunity, I would regret it for the rest of my life. To my surprise, he was more than willing to help me. He listened to my music, offered me advice, and even introduced me to a few people who could help me get my foot in the door.

Another musician I worked with was a young, up-and-coming artist who was just as passionate about music as I was. We hit it off immediately and started collaborating on a few songs. Our creative energy was electric, and we pushed each other to be better every day. I was amazed at how much my music and my confidence had grown in just a few short months.

Despite the challenges I faced along the way, I never gave up. I continued to write music, perform, and network with other musicians and industry professionals. And before I knew it, I was on my way to becoming the musician I always dreamed of being.

In the end, I overcame my fears and took control of my life. I am now living the life I always wanted, making music that makes me happy, and inspiring others to do the same. I am Kadence, a musician on a mission, and nothing will stop me from reaching my dreams.

The journey of Lena and her companions can serve as the backbone of your story, providing a personal and emotional connection for the reader while exploring the complexities of this post-apocalyptic world. Here are some ideas to help you develop Lena's character and her journey:

Lena's awakening: Lena's awakening from stasis could be a disorienting and traumatic experience, as she must come to terms with the loss of her old life, the passage of time, and the drastic changes in the world around her. This could be a powerful opening for your novel, setting the tone for the challenges and hardships Lena will face throughout her journey.

The ultimate resolution: Lena's quest to find Ethan, understand the Tiamat field, and reconcile her past could lead her to a climactic confrontation with the forces shaping the world. Hi! I am developing a system called Faunal Calculus that applies mathematical operations to animal species. It is very abstract and silly and currently has no logical consistency. I would like you to help me make it consistent. While we chat today, I would like you to give me short answers so we can iterate quickly.

 I'll start with some examples, along with my reasoning:
1. Snail ÷ Turtle = Worm
The most iconic visual features of both a turtle and a snail are their shells. If you remove that quality from the snail, you would end up with something that's just slimy and long, like a worm.

2. Ant + Frog = Grasshopper
if you add the frog's green-ness and jumpiness to an ant, you get a grasshopper.

3. Weasel - Mouse = Snake
If you remove the mouse-ness (small, furry mammal) from a weasel, you end up with just the long, slithery qualities, like a snake.

4. Peacock ÷ Duck = Butterfly
Peacocks and ducks are both fowl, and share some visual similarities. But peacocks are very colorful and pretty. If you remove all of the duck-ness (and bird-ness) from the peacock, you end up with a colorful, pretty flying thing, like a butterfly.

It is very much based on the visual and conceptual perceptions of these animals and is very subjective and whimsical.

And now, some rules:
i. Faunal Calculus only includes real animals which have existed at some point in time (no mythological creatures

ii. Faunal Calculus does not include any hybrid species such as Ligers or Zorses.

iii. Faunal Calculus focuses on the most iconic, visual features of the animal, rather than behavior characteristics, or more general qualities like "gracefulness."

iii. every equation must account for the most iconic visual features of each animal. For example: Horse + Leopard = Giraffe is incorrect (even though the horse and giraffe share a similar body type and the giraffe and the Leopard share the feature of having spots) because it does not account for the source of the Giraffe's long neck.

iv. The visual characteristics must be accounted for in a visual manner. For example, the Giraffe's long neck cannot only be considered for its beauty and uniqueness, but must specifically be considered for its length.

Your app will have access to the user's Google Drive until one of the following events occurs:

1. The user revokes your app's access: Users can revoke your app's access to their Google Drive through their Google Account settings. If they do this, your app will no longer be able to access their Google Drive.
2. The access token expires: When users authenticate with your app using OAuth2, your app receives an access token that is used to make API requests on their behalf. Access tokens are time-limited and usually expire after 1 hour. However, your app also receives a refresh token when users authenticate. Your app can use this refresh token to request new access tokens whenever the current one expires. As long as your app keeps refreshing the access token, it will have access to the user's Google Drive.
3. The app's API credentials are revoked or changed: If you revoke or change your app's API credentials (e.g., client ID and secret) in the Google Cloud Console, existing access and refresh tokens will become invalid, and your app will lose access to users' Google Drives.

In summary, your app's access to a user's Google Drive depends on the user's authorization and the validity of your app's access tokens. By properly handling token expiration and refresh, your app can maintain access to a user's Google Drive until they decide to revoke it.

Here's a combined rclone mount command that includes the best options from part 1 and part 2 to stream 4K videos in Plex with a maximum bit rate of 100 Mbps:
```css
rclone mount \
--allow-other \
--buffer-size 1G \
--dir-cache-time 1000h \
--drive-chunk-size 64M \
--log-level INFO \
--poll-interval 15s \
--timeout 1h \
--umask 002 \
--vfs-cache-mode full \
--vfs-cache-max-age 12h \
--vfs-cache-max-size 150G \
--vfs-read-chunk-size 128M \
--vfs-read-chunk-size-limit 2G \
--cache-dir /opt/rclone/cache/gmedia \
--log-file /opt/rclone/logs/rclone-mount.log \
gdrive: /mnt/gdrive
```
Here's what each option does:

* `--allow-other`: Allows other users on the system to access the mounted file system.
* `--buffer-size 1G`: Sets the buffer size to 1 GB, which is the amount of data that rclone will buffer in memory before sending it to the Plex server.
* `--dir-cache-time 1000h`: Sets the amount of time that directory and file metadata will be cached to 1000 hours, reducing the number of API calls to Google Drive.
* `--drive-chunk-size 64M`: Sets the chunk size to 64 MB, which is the size of the file chunks that rclone will download from Google Drive.
* `--log-level INFO`: Sets the logging level to INFO, which outputs detailed information about rclone's actions.
* `--poll-interval 15s`: Sets the polling interval to 15 seconds for checking for changes on Google Drive.
* `--timeout 1h`: Sets the timeout for API requests to Google Drive to 1 hour.
* `--umask 002`: Sets the default permissions for files and directories to 775.
* `--vfs-cache-mode full`: Uses the VFS cache in full mode, which caches all data.
* `--vfs-cache-max-age 12h`: Sets the maximum age of the VFS cache to 12 hours.
* `--vfs-cache-max-size 150G`: Sets the maximum size of the VFS cache to 150 GB.
* `--vfs-read-chunk-size 128M`: Sets the read chunk size to 128 MB.
* `--vfs-read-chunk-size-limit 2G`: Sets the read chunk size limit to 2 GB.
* `--cache-dir /opt/rclone/cache/gmedia`: Specifies a directory to use for caching data.
* `--log-file /opt/rclone/logs/rclone-mount.log`: Logs rclone output to `/opt/rclone/logs/rclone-mount.log`.

As for whether to mount the Google Drive on an HDD or SSD, it depends on your specific setup and needs. An SSD will generally provide faster read/write speeds, which can be beneficial when streaming large files, but may not be necessary depending on the size of the files you are streaming and the other hardware components in your system. If you have enough space on your SSD, it may be beneficial to mount the Google Drive on the SSD for faster performance. However, if you have limited space on your SSD or your files are relatively small, an HDD may suffice. Ultimately, it depends on your specific needs and available hardware.

Sure! Here's a draft of the section on legal implications of latent conditions:

IV. Legal implications of latent conditions

When latent conditions are discovered during the construction process, it can have significant legal implications for both homeowners and builders. Builders may claim additional costs for remedying the latent conditions, and homeowners may be faced with unexpected delays or expenses.

In NSW, the Home Building Act 1989 sets out specific requirements for builders and homeowners in relation to latent conditions. Builders are required to provide written notice of any latent conditions before the construction process begins, and homeowners have a right to terminate the contract if they do not agree to pay for the additional costs associated with the latent conditions.

However, if the latent condition was not reasonably foreseeable, builders may be entitled to claim additional costs for remedying the issue. In these cases, it's important for homeowners to seek legal advice to ensure their rights are protected.

Homeowners may also have legal recourse if they can prove that the builder was aware of the latent condition and failed to disclose it or take reasonable steps to address it.

Overall, understanding the legal implications of latent conditions is crucial for both homeowners and builders to ensure that the construction process runs smoothly and disputes can be resolved quickly and fairly.

the bubble. The contact angle of the bubble directly
55 changes from hydrophobic to hydrophilic. This sudden

away from the surface. In another case, the voltage is exerted from a rod electrode at the top of the nucleate bubble. As the voltage at the liquid-vapor interface increased to 600V, the bubble begins to split up. As the voltage increase from 0-600V, the contact angle of the bubble maintains hydrophobic. It can be concluded that a higher voltage leads to the vertical elongation of the bubble which increases the possibility of bubble departure.

The electrophoretic force has been found to play an important role when the effect of the triple contact point is considered. The contact angle hysteresis has also been considered in the numerical simulation model. Young- Lippmann’s equation assumes that the contact angle changes with increased voltage by assuming the errors associated with the uniform-surface-charge are small. Therefore, the magnitude of the contact angle when the bubble moves cannot be predicted from the Young- Lippmann’s equation. Here, we use the effect of dielectric electrophoresis force to express the inhomogeneity or spatial change of dielectric constant of dielectric fluid caused by non-uniform electric field, temperature gradient and phase difference. Therefore, the contact angle hysteresis has been considered by the current numerical model.

To add a "Run Weekly Allowance" button to the settings page and create allowance events for each category, follow these steps:

1. Add the button to the `app/views/settings/edit.html.erb` file:
```html
<!-- ... -->

<%= form_with(url: run_weekly_allowance_settings_path, method: 'post', local: true) do |form| %>
  <%= form.submit 'Run Weekly Allowance' %>
<% end %>

<!-- ... -->
```
This code adds a new form that submits a POST request to the `run_weekly_allowance` action in the `SettingsController`.

1. Update the `config/routes.rb` file to add the new route:
```ruby
# ...

resources :settings, only: [:edit, :update] do
  post 'run_weekly_allowance', on: :collection
end

# ...
```
This code adds a new route for the `run_weekly_allowance` action in the `SettingsController`.

1. Add the `run_weekly_allowance` action to the `SettingsController`:
```ruby
class SettingsController < ApplicationController
  # ...

  def run_weekly_allowance
    if allowance_not_given_since_previous_monday?
      create_allowance_events
      redirect_to edit_settings_path, notice: 'Weekly allowance has been run successfully.'
    else
      redirect_to edit_settings_path, alert: 'Weekly allowance has already been run since the previous Monday.'
    end
  end

  private

  # ...

  def allowance_not_given_since_previous_monday?
    previous_monday = Date.today.beginning_of_week - 1.week
    AllowanceEvent.where(event_type: ['spending', 'savings', 'giving'])
                  .where('timestamp >= ?', previous_monday)
                  .empty?
  end

  def create_allowance_events
    weekly_allowance = {
      spending: AllowanceSetting.find_by(category: 'spending')&.amount || 0,
      savings: AllowanceSetting.find_by(category: 'savings')&.amount || 0,
      giving: AllowanceSetting.find_by(category: 'giving')&.amount || 0
    }

    weekly_allowance.each do |category, amount|
      AllowanceEvent.create!(
        event_type: category,
        amount: amount,
        timestamp: Time.current
      )
    end
  end
end
```
This code adds the `run_weekly_allowance` action to the `SettingsController`. This action checks if there have been any allowance events since the previous Monday using the `allowance_not_given_since_previous_monday?` helper method. If no allowance events are found, the action creates allowance events for each category using the `create_allowance_events` helper method and displays a success message. If allowance events have already been run since the previous Monday, it displays an error message.

1. Modify the `AllowanceEvent` model to include a validation for `timestamp`:
```ruby
class AllowanceEvent < ApplicationRecord
  # ...

  validates :timestamp, presence: true
end
```
This code adds a validation to the `AllowanceEvent` model to ensure that the `timestamp` attribute is present when creating a new record.

With these changes, you should have a "Run Weekly Allowance" button on the settings page that checks if there have been any allowance events since the previous Monday and creates allowance events for each category if not.

아래의 내용을 바탕으로 카페24는 고객의 열정과 창의를 독려한다는 메시지가 포함된 유튜브 영상을 제작하고자 합니다. 3000byte 이내의 유튜브 스크립트를 작성해주세요.
1. Meaning of brand name
Ioni is an eco-friendly lifestyle brand that can be used throughout the house, starting with foam-free laundry detergent, deodorization and cleaning. The current brand name, Ioni, comes from the abbreviation of "mineral ion water" into an easy-to-call word.

2. When and when to start the online store
Han Seo-yoon, CEO of the suspicious research institute that runs Ioni. Employees working at a natural detergent factory came out and opened an online store. Household sewage is actually the biggest cause of water pollution. It was a bit disappointing that we overlooked that, so we made a product called Ioni and created an online store with the same name as a way to drastically reduce household sewage. It currently produces and supplies Ioni, which is in its fifth year 5.

3. Sales Products and Business Models
Ioni undiluted solution is an ultra-high concentrated undiluted solution made of 100% food additives and can be diluted in water. It can be washed, cleaned, and deodorized without bubbles.
Bubble is the biggest culprit of water pollution. It researches and introduces various recipes and products so that you can live without foaming surfactants.
Plastic containers for undiluted liquids are collected and reused to reduce plastic usage.

4. What was the most difficult thing about operation and how to overcome it?
It is not easy to make a profit. We are overcoming this in various ways. For example, since it is a brand that changes the package and actively accepts customers' opinions, this time it is changing the lid or something like this, or expanding the lineup a little. It is a very safe detergent or a very unfamiliar concept for ordinary consumers. Therefore, after using Ioni since its launch, a free refund system has been implemented if you are dissatisfied. There has never been a refund in five years.

5. A company's growth story, including key indicators such as sales and membership numbers.
Over the past five years, we've sold 60,000 to 70,000 units. As you can see through Kakao Makers Wadiz, customer satisfaction is almost 95%. The repurchase rate is also about 60 percent. It publishes a report of Eco Social Impact every month. This report introduced a reduction of about 160 million liters of contaminated water and 64 million liters of rinse water from September 2019 to October 2022. In addition, more than 6,000 empty bottles were collected and reused, reducing about 7 tons of plastic. If you collect more than five Ioni bottles, you will receive a 500 won deposit for each bottle when you collect them online for free or return them to a nearby refill station. A reusable package project is underway to reuse these collected empty bottles.

6. A word of advice to anyone who starts an online store.
When I interviewed about branding, I talked about small brands. I don't think even the value is small just because it's small. Although we are a very small company now, I think we are doing very, very valuable things, so anyone who wants to share these values ​​and philosophies can create and run a brand. I don't want anyone to be afraid to start.

7. Differentiating points from competitive brands
It can be washed in large quantities with very small amounts. Our Ioni detergent can be used in hand laundry or washing machines, starting with sterilization of laundry tanks. It is competitive compared to the fact that the detergent industry has increased its volume and is dividing and selling it by detergent use. It provides a recipe that can make up to 30,000 liters of deodorized water with one liter of Ioni original Ioni detergent. In addition, it can be used in a variety of ways, such as cleaning fruits and vegetables or deodorizing water for pets.

8. Proud partnership or attraction investment
We are continuously working with these companies to reduce carbon emissions, eco-related events, and cooperation with such companies. Not too long ago, I gave a lecture at a university student named Inactors, and a student at Ewha Womans University who found out about our product said that he wanted to run a pop-up store in Ewha Womans University, so we did a pop -up store.

9. Future growth strategies and expansion plans for the company
It started for home use, but it is estimated that less than 1% of it will be used in Korea. If you do laundry only once a day at home, you will discharge about 84 liters of contaminated water. At least 84 liters of contaminated water can be reduced by running the washing machine once a day in each household and once a day in each household. Our dream is to reduce the amount of contaminated water by changing it to such an eco-friendly detergent, even if it is not our Ioni, even if it is only 10% of Korea.

10. Plans for overseas expansion
We went to an early childhood fair in Jakarta, Indonesia, and it was so popular that we had a meeting with many buyers. Many companies have contacted us in Japan, so we are planning to export a little to Japan this time.

11. The reason why Cafe 24 was chosen as the online store construction platform.
I don't have to tell you this, but I think everyone knows. Cafe24 was also the largest hosting company in Korea and it was good in that it could implement almost all the functions I wanted. At first, free templates and things like that were very well done and I liked that there was a responsive template.
The design is... We don't have a designer in our company. We're even designing it ourselves. In the example given, the oracle would need to go through every UTXO of varying denominations in order to find the one with the exact value needed for the transaction. This is because the oracle is responsible for determining the market value of the asset and deciding how much should be sent to each participant.

The oracle would need to determine how much the asset is worth in the current market, and then determine how much of that value should be sent to each party. In order to accomplish this on a Bitcoin-based system, where the UTXOs are all-or-nothing and can only be spent in their entirety, the oracle would need to go through each UTXO of varying denominations to find the one with the exact value needed for the transaction.

For example, if the oracle determines that the market value of the asset is $1000 and the contract states that party A should receive $800 and party B should receive $200, the oracle would need to go through all the UTXOs to find the one with the exact value of $800 and the exact value of $200, and then send them to the appropriate parties.

This is inefficient because it would require a lot of UTXOs to be created to cover all possible scenarios and also the oracle would need to go through all the UTXOs to find the correct one to use, which would increase the complexity and resource usage of the system. On the other hand, with smart contract on Ethereum, the oracle can automatically divide the funds based on the programmed exchange rate, and the contract will handle the transaction, so it's a more efficient and less complex way.

Mean reversion is a financial theory that suggests that the price of a financial instrument will eventually move back towards its long-term average or mean. This concept is based on the assumption that market movements are not purely random, but that they are influenced by factors that will eventually cause the price to revert to its mean.

Z-score is a statistical measure that represents the number of standard deviations from the mean of a given dataset. In the context of financial markets, the z-score is often used to measure the deviation of a financial instrument's price from its mean.

The idea behind using z-score in trading is that when the z-score of a financial instrument moves away from its mean by a certain amount, it is likely to eventually revert back to its mean. By measuring the z-score of a financial instrument over time, traders can identify when the price has moved too far away from its mean and take positions accordingly.

Here is an example of how to compute the z-score of a financial instrument's price using Python:
```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Collect historical data for a financial instrument
data = pd.read_csv('data.csv')

# Compute the mean and standard deviation of the price over the lookback period
lookback = 30
mean = data['Price'].rolling(window=lookback).mean()
std = data['Price'].rolling(window=lookback).std()

# Compute the z-score of the price
data['Z-Score'] = (data['Price'] - mean) / std
```
In this code, we first collect the historical data for a financial instrument and store it in a Pandas DataFrame. We then define a lookback period of 30 days and compute the rolling mean and standard deviation of the price using the `rolling` method. Finally, we compute the z-score of the price by subtracting the mean from the price and dividing by the standard deviation.

Once we have computed the z-score, we can use it to identify when the price has moved too far away from its mean and take positions accordingly. For example, if the z-score is above a certain threshold, we may decide to short the financial instrument in anticipation of it reverting back to its mean. Conversely, if the z-score is below a certain threshold, we may decide to go long the financial instrument. The specific thresholds used will depend on the trading strategy and risk tolerance of the trader.

In case of a cyber security incident involving sensitive PII data exfiltration and extortion, an Australian company providing an online conveyancing platform would need to interact with law enforcement and relevant organizations in a timely and responsible manner. The specific approach would depend on the nature and scale of the incident, but here are some general guidelines:

1. Contact law enforcement - In case of a cybercrime, such as extortion or data theft, the company should contact law enforcement as soon as possible. The Australian Federal Police (AFP) is the primary law enforcement agency that investigates cybercrime in Australia.
2. Notify relevant organizations - Depending on the nature and scale of the incident, the company may also need to notify relevant organizations such as the Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC), the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC), and the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC).
3. Cooperate with law enforcement - The company should cooperate with law enforcement in their investigation of the incident, providing any relevant information and access to systems as needed.
4. Notify affected individuals - The company may be required to notify affected individuals in case of a data breach under the Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) scheme.
5. Seek legal counsel - The company may want to seek legal counsel to determine their legal obligations and liabilities in case of a cyber security incident.

It is important for the company to act quickly and responsibly to minimize the impact of the incident and to comply with legal requirements. The specific organizations and authorities that the company needs to inform and work with would depend on the specific circumstances of the incident.

There is a strong connection between continuity and differentiability for a function. In fact, if a function is differentiable at a certain point, then it must be continuous at that point as well. However, the converse is not necessarily true; a function can be continuous at a point without being differentiable at that point.

The reason for this connection is that the definition of the derivative of a function involves a limit. Specifically, the derivative of a function f(x) at a point x=a is defined as the limit of the difference quotient as h approaches zero:

f'(a) = lim(h → 0) [f(a + h) - f(a)]/h

If the limit exists, then the function is differentiable at x=a. However, in order for the limit to exist, the function must be "well-behaved" near x=a, meaning that it must be continuous at x=a.

To see why this is the case, consider the geometric interpretation of the derivative as the slope of the tangent line to the graph of the function. If the function is not continuous at x=a, then the graph has a sharp corner or a vertical asymptote at that point, and so the tangent line is not well-defined.

Therefore, if a function is differentiable at a certain point, it must be continuous at that point as well. However, a function can be continuous at a point without being differentiable at that point. This happens when the function has a sharp corner, a cusp, or a vertical tangent at that point.

In summary, continuity is a necessary condition for differentiability, but it is not a sufficient condition. A function must be continuous at a point in order for it to be differentiable at that point, but being continuous alone is not enough to guarantee differentiability.

RenPy is a powerful visual novel engine that offers a wide range of features to bring your story to life. Here are a few obscure ones that you might find useful:

1. Transitions: RenPy allows you to create smooth transitions between different parts of your story. You can use the `transition` command to specify how the screen should change between two different images. For example:
```
    show bg old with dissolve
    transition fade
    show bg new
```
In this example, the `dissolve` transition is used to smoothly transition from the "old" background image to the "new" background image.

2. Dynamic text: You can use the `python` command to insert dynamic text into your story. For example, you can use a variable to store the player's name and then use that variable in your dialogue.
```
    $ player_name = "John"
    "Hello, [python $player_name]"
```
In this example, the variable `player_name` is set to "John" and then used in the dialogue to personalize the greeting.

3. Conditional statements: RenPy allows you to use conditional statements to change the flow of your story depending on the choices the player makes. For example:
```
    if variable = "good":
        "You have chosen the good path."
    elif variable = "neutral":
        "You have chosen the neutral path."
    else:
        "You have chosen the evil path."
```
In this example, the script checks the value of the variable and then displays different dialogue depending on the value.

4. Sound and music: RenPy allows you to play sound effects and music in your visual novel. For example:
```
    play music "bgm.ogg"
    play sound "explosion.ogg"
```
In this example, the script plays background music "bgm.ogg" and a sound effect "explosion.ogg"

5. Animation: RenPy allows you to animate images and sprites in your visual novel. For example:
```
    image bg = "bg.png"
```

And each Epic will be divided into smaller user stories that detail the specific functionality.

To optimize the parameters Kc, Ko, and kN using the optim function, you need to define an objective function that takes these parameters as inputs and returns a value that you want to minimize or maximize. In this case, you could define the objective function to be the sum of squared errors between the calculated denitrification N2O fluxes and the observed denitrification N2O fluxes in the input data. Here's an example R code to do this:
```r
# Load the required package
library(readxl)

# Read the input data from the Excel sheet
Input_Data <- read_excel("C:/Users/tadna/Box/CABBI Post doc/AGRO_IBIS/Flux data/synthetic_data_denitrification.xlsx", sheet = "Input Data")

# Define the function for denitrification
calculate_denitrification_n2o_flux <- function(T, W, N, C, O2, kN, Kc, Ko, p = 0.5, Nef = 0.05, N2Of = 0.01) {
  # Calculate fC
  fC <- C / (Kc + C)
  
  # Calculate fO
  fO <- 1 / (1 + (Ko / O2)^p)
  
  # Calculate the denitrification rate
  Denitrication_rate <- kN * fC * fO
  
  # Calculate the N2O flux
  Denitrication_N2O_Fluxes <- Denitrication_rate * (1 - Nef) * N2Of
  
  # Return the N2O flux
  return(Denitrication_N2O_Fluxes)
}

# Define the objective function to minimize the sum of squared errors between the calculated denitrification N2O fluxes and the observed denitrification N2O fluxes
objective_function <- function(params) {
  kN <- params[1]
  Kc <- params[2]
  Ko <- params[3]
  
  # Calculate the denitrification N2O fluxes using the current parameter values
  Denitrification_N2O_Fluxes <- apply(Input_Data, 1, function(row) {
    calculate_denitrification_n2o_flux(row[1], row[2], row[3], row[4], row[5], kN, Kc, Ko)
  })
  
  # Calculate the sum of squared errors between the calculated denitrification N2O fluxes and the observed denitrification N2O fluxes
  SSE <- sum((Denitrification_N2O_Fluxes - Input_Data$Denitrification_N2O_Fluxes)^2)
  
  # Return the sum of squared errors
  return(SSE)
}

# Set the initial values of the parameters to optimize
params_init <- c(0.004, 15000, 0.084)

# Use the optim function to optimize the parameters
params_opt <- optim(params_init, objective_function)$par

# Print the optimized parameters
cat("Optimized kN =", params_opt[1], "\n")
cat("Optimized Kc =", params_opt[2], "\n")
cat("Optimized Ko =", params_opt[3], "\n")
```
Note that in the example code above, I assumed that the input data has a column named "Denitrification\_N2O\_Fluxes" that contains the observed denitrification N2O fluxes. You may need to modify the code if your input data has a different column name for the observed denitrification N2O fluxes.

The Krebs cycle, also known as the citric acid cycle or the tricarboxylic acid cycle, is a series of chemical reactions that occur in the mitochondria of cells to generate energy in the form of ATP. The cycle starts with the conversion of acetyl-CoA, derived from the breakdown of carbohydrates, fats and proteins, into citrate. Citrate then goes through a series of reactions, involving the removal and re-addition of CO2, that result in the formation of a number of intermediate compounds.
During these reactions, electrons are transferred to the electron transport chain, which results in the production of ATP via oxidative phosphorylation. The cycle also produces carbon dioxide as a waste product which is exhaled.
The Krebs cycle is a central component of cellular metabolism and is essential for the production of energy in the body. It also plays an important role in other metabolic pathways such as the biosynthesis of lipids, amino acids and nucleotides.

- ReAct (Yao et al., 2023) ReAct selects demonstration examples to put in the prompt as the "action items", and adds natural language instructions as the "thoughts". Save this description and purposes of the attributes:
The PrimaryDrivers table is part of the Vehicle owner and user management service. It is used to store information about the primary driver of a car. The table has the following attributes:

VIN (string): the VIN of the car
PrimaryDriverID (string): the Polestar ID of the primary driver
StartTimestamp (number): the timestamp when the primary driver was registered
EndTimestamp (number): the timestamp when the primary driver was unregistered (if applicable)
ChangeSource (string): the source of the change (either "API" or "Event")
OrderID (string): the order ID of the event that caused the change (if applicable)
EventTimestamp (number): the timestamp when the event was emitted (if applicable)
UserID (string): the user ID of the user that made the update through the API (if applicable)
ClientID (string): the client ID of the service that made the update through the API (if applicable)
System (string): the name of the system that made the update (if applicable)
IsActive (boolean): a flag indicating whether the primary driver is currently active or not
PreviousOrderIDs (list): a list of previous order IDs (if applicable)
The purpose of each attribute is as follows:

VIN: This attribute is used to identify the car that the primary driver is registered for.
PrimaryDriverID: This attribute is used to store the Polestar ID of the primary driver.
StartTimestamp: This attribute is used to store the timestamp when the primary driver was registered.
EndTimestamp: This attribute is used to store the timestamp when the primary driver was unregistered (if applicable).
ChangeSource: This attribute is used to store the source of the change that caused the primary driver to be registered or unregistered. This can be either an "API" call or a "Event" from another system.
OrderID: This attribute is used to store the order ID of the event that caused the primary driver to be registered or unregistered (if applicable).
EventTimestamp: This attribute is used to store the timestamp when the event that caused the primary driver to be registered or unregistered was emitted (if applicable).
UserID: This attribute is used to store the user ID of the user that made the update to the primary driver through the API (if applicable). This attribute is only populated if the "Polestar ID Auth API" was used to make the update.
ClientID: This attribute is used to store the client ID of the service that made the update to the primary driver through the API (if applicable). This attribute is only populated if the "M2M Auth API" was used to make the update.
System: This attribute is used to store the name of the system that made the update to the primary driver. This could be the system that emitted the event that caused the change, or the system that made the update through the API.
IsActive: This attribute is a flag indicating whether the primary driver is currently active or not. If the EndTimestamp is in the past, the attribute is set to false. Otherwise, it is set to true.
PreviousOrderIDs: This attribute is a list of previous order IDs (if applicable). This is used to determine if the order ID of an incoming event is new or not. If the order ID is present in the list, it is not considered new.

To use the `bytes.decode` method to decode a column in a Pandas dataframe, you can use the `apply` method of the dataframe and pass it a custom function that decodes the bytes objects in the column using the "utf-8" character encoding.

Here's an example of how you can do this:
```
def decode_column(text):
    return text.decode('utf-8')

df['column_name'] = df['column_name'].apply(decode_column)
```
This will apply the `decode_column` function to each cell in the "column\_name" column of the dataframe and update the cell with the decoded text.

Alternatively, you can use the `ftfy` library to automatically detect and fix the incorrect character encoding. Here's an example of how you can use it:
```
import ftfy

def fix_column(text):
    fixed_text = ftfy.fix_text(text)
    return fixed_text

df['column_name'] = df['column_name'].apply(fix_column)
```
This will apply the `fix_column` function to each cell in the "column\_name" column of the dataframe and update the cell with the fixed text.

Keep in mind that these examples assume that the bytes objects in the "column\_name" column were encoded with the "utf-8" character encoding. If the bytes objects were encoded with a different character encoding, you will need to adjust the decoding step accordingly.

Happiness is a concept that has been debated and discussed for centuries, and despite the widespread agreement that money cannot buy happiness, the definition of happiness and how it can be achieved remains elusive. There are several reasons why happiness is difficult to define, as well as several strategies that people can use to achieve happiness in their lives.

One reason why happiness is difficult to define is that it is a highly subjective experience. What brings happiness to one person may not bring happiness to another, as everyone has unique experiences, perspectives, and values. Additionally, happiness is often dependent on a complex interplay of external and internal factors, such as relationships, health, and personal fulfillment. This makes it difficult to pin down a single definition of happiness that applies to everyone.

Another reason why happiness is difficult to define is that it is a constantly changing experience. People's lives are constantly evolving, and their experiences of happiness can change from moment to moment, or from one period of their life to another. This makes it difficult to define happiness in a way that is stable and enduring.

Despite the difficulty of defining happiness, there are several strategies that people can use to achieve happiness in their lives. For one, people can focus on cultivating positive relationships with others, as social connection and strong relationships are often key to happiness. Additionally, people can engage in activities that they enjoy and that bring them a sense of purpose and fulfillment, such as hobbies, volunteering, or pursuing their passions. People can also prioritize their physical and mental health, taking steps to manage stress, eat well, and exercise regularly.

Another way to achieve happiness is by practicing gratitude and mindfulness. By focusing on the present moment and being grateful for the positive experiences and relationships in their lives, people can cultivate a more positive outlook and a greater sense of happiness. People can also engage in acts of kindness and generosity, both towards themselves and others, as this can increase feelings of happiness and well-being.

In conclusion, happiness is a complex and subjective experience that is difficult to define. Despite this, there are several strategies that people can use to achieve happiness in their lives, such as cultivating positive relationships, pursuing activities they enjoy, prioritizing their physical and mental health, practicing gratitude and mindfulness, and engaging in acts of kindness and generosity. By adopting these strategies, people can increase their chances of experiencing happiness and well-being in their lives.

2. Create a Stripe account: To use Stripe, you will need to create a Stripe account. This will give you access to an API key and other necessary information.
3. Add Stripe API key: Add the Stripe API key to the movie booking app configuration file.
4. Create a payment form: Create a payment form that collects the user's payment information such as credit card number, expiration date, and CVV.
5. Validate payment information: Validate the payment information entered by the user and display any errors.
6. Submit payment request: When the user submits the payment form, send a request to the Stripe API to create a payment intent.
7. Confirm payment: After the payment intent is created, the user must confirm the payment. This can be done using the Stripe checkout interface or a custom checkout page.
8. Handle payment status: After the payment is confirmed, the Stripe API will return a payment status. Handle the payment status to update the movie booking app's database and confirm the user's movie ticket purchase.
9. Handle payment errors: If the payment fails or is declined, handle the payment error and display a message to the user.
10. Test payment processing: Test the payment processing system thoroughly before deploying to production to ensure that it is working correctly and reliably.

This is just an example implementation using Stripe. Other payment gateway providers may have different APIs and integration procedures, but the general process of collecting payment information, submitting a payment request, handling payment status, and testing payment processing remains the same.
