the essay: Far East of New York in Bermuda lies one of North America’s most influential nations and island countries, New Pangea. The country holds promising social achievements as well as many economic and technological accomplishments that have been recognized internationally for nearly centuries. 

The island country was once exclusively inhabited by its natives referred to as “Kraenons”. Despite their lack of appearance in any historical events with other countries before their English encounters, the Kraenons were fully capable of creating their own technology and advanced mathematics (Though interestingly, there have been no accounts of the Kraenons seeking out foreign civilization before they were discovered). After merging a large majority of the population with English settlers over nearly a century, a newly formed Pangean culture would take hold of the country. Pangean culture amalgamates many aspects of 1600s English and Kraenon culture such as the Kraenon “Bristle week” and English “Christmas”. Pangeans hold a deeply devotional respect for birds, it is not uncommon to find residents of the country preparing bird seeds for native birds to feast. Over the 20th century, New Pangea experienced massive influence from western and European cultures, especially in technology and architecture such as the popularity of the telephone and television as well as homes with white exteriors and rectangular windows. Among the many cultural practices in New Pangea, one of the most followed and respected values in families and communities across the country are the benefits and regard for education, education is commonly viewed as the only path to success within the general population. New Pangea’s educational philosophy is taken seriously even by the youth of the country who view subjects such as math or science as challenging entertainment rather than a forced subject of no interest. 

Due to New Pangea's deep cultural beliefs in education, schools across the country receive up to 20% of the country’s yearly budget, or up to 100,000,000 PAN (125,000,000 CAD). Due to New Pangea's comparatively high wealth:population ratio when compared to other developed countries, students are offered much higher quality education from much more qualified educators. Many classes in Pangean schools are held outdoors to maintain the mental state of students while they learn, homework in the country is also regularly minimized to only long-term projects for certain subjects, keeping students from stress and allowing them to enjoy their time outside of school as well as giving them a task to complete over time. As of 2022, New Pangea’s education has been successful enough to maintain a 1250 SAT score average as well as a 84% college/university graduation rate among the population above the age of 24.

As well as high cultural regard for education, families in New Pangea show respect through a variety of customs and practices. Pangeans are expected to bow when greeted in consideration of others, especially their parents or the elderly. It is also expected that Pangeans respect and obey their parents. In addition to many practices of social courtesy and belief, Pangeans follow a strict philosophy of respect for their natural environment. Socially unaccepted yet foreignly common bad habits in this regard such as littering or vandalizing are disputed as contentious and controversial, this etiquette is largely believed to be owed to the nation's vast reverence for birds of the country’s wild. The population's cultural connection with nature has also given a noticeable impact on the laws in the country. In 2008, the New Pangea National Congress would pass the “New Atomica” bill, which would successfully demolish the use of fossil fuels nationwide by 97.4% by 2015, the countries main source of energy since and before has been nuclear energy sourced from Atomica Industries, raising safety concerns which have yet to be dealt with. In 2014, New Pangea National Congress would then pass the “Green Pangea” bill which would mandate recycling bins to be put across the country, as well as basic education on recycling waste in both public and private schools.

The living standards of New Pangea are comparably higher in comparison with other developed nations. All youth are legally required and provided to participate in the country’s education which has been ranked the highest globally by organizations such as the Global Citizens For Human Rights. The social democratic system implemented into New Pangea’s economy allows even the lowest financial classes of the country to be provided with a healthy amount of food and water as well as shelter and healthcare all without the burden of financial stress. Because of these factors, the people of New Pangea live comfortable lives where their health is almost always independent of their class. Children in the country do not have to work to provide for their families financially, allowing children in New Pangea to be given time for study, creativity and entertainment. Adults in New Pangea are driven to work by and for the nation’s capitalist economy in order to afford the luxuries (food, homes, technology…) provided by the economy. For the less fortunate, the government provides centralized but rationed food, water and shelter as well as fully covered healthcare. Though it is more common to live in privately owned properties, New Pangea’s government offers tax-paid shelters called “Government Residential Buildings” to the homeless population, though these homes are not considered luxurious or even comfortable for some, it meets the mental and physical standards for those who choose to live in them. The typical middle class worker in New Pangea works from morning (9-11AM) to evening (6-9PM) and is paid on average 24.5 CAD an hour, more than enough to be both financially stable and mentally comfortable in the country’s economy.

The economy of New Pangea is fueled by the country’s 3 big industries, the Seafood industry, Mining industry and Technology industry. Just like other Island countries, New Pangea is the home of some of the world's most exotic and profitable seafood businesses, namely “Jontora seafoods”, the world's largest seafood company which made approximately 45,000,000,000 CAD in 2022 (Though the company originated in New Pangea, it has many sister locations put internationally). The Seafood industry in New Pangea also contributes to the country’s alluring tourist industry which made 70,000,000,000 CAD in 2022. The biggest driving factors of New Pangea’s economic growth are the country’s mining and technology industry. Since the 1960s, Atomica Industries (which is government owned) has been mining tonnes of uranium among other valuable minerals in Morriloe Island, creating a net profit of 400,176,100 CAD from mining alone in 2022. Atomica Industries as well as other Pangean companies such as Mei-Plye-technologies have accounted for the country’s technological advancements in Nuclear technology, Medical technology and AI/computer technology. Technological exports from the country made net profit of approximately 1,000,000,000,000 CAD in only 2022, this is largely owed to the country’s abundance of metal from Morriloe Island as well as a national emphasis on education. In recent years, technology has seen a steep improvement worldwide, opening an opportunity for the already thriving tech industry of New Pangea to blossom in now growing fields such as quantum computing, AI technology and more.

In Python, code running from the same interpreter can import and use loggers that have been created or configured in other parts of the code. This allows different parts of the code to share and use the same logger instance, which can simplify logging and make it easier to manage and configure log output.

For example, if an engineer creates a logger in the main part of their code, they can import and use that logger in a separate function or module that is called by the main code. This allows the code running in the separate function or module to use the same logger instance, which means that log messages will be written to the same output and use the same configuration as the logger in the main code.

Overall, code running from the same interpreter can import and use loggers that have been created or configured in other parts of the code, which can simplify logging and improve the management of log output.

A Model of MIS System is an organized framework that represents the components and processes involved in a Management Information System. It is a blueprint that shows how data is collected, processed, and transformed into information, which is used to make informed decisions in an organization. In this answer, we will discuss the various components of the MIS system and how they work together to create an effective MIS model.

1. Input

Input is the first component of the MIS system. It involves gathering data from various sources, such as internal and external sources. The data can be in different forms, such as raw data, transactions, and documents. Input can be manual, such as data entry, or automated, such as sensors, scanners, and machines. The input data must be accurate, timely, and relevant to be useful for decision-making.

1. Processing

Processing is the second component of the MIS system. It involves converting the input data into meaningful information. Processing can be manual or automated, depending on the complexity of the data and the requirements of the organization. Processing involves various activities, such as sorting, classifying, summarizing, and analyzing data. The processed information must be accurate, consistent, and complete to be useful for decision-making.

1. Storage

Storage is the third component of the MIS system. It involves storing the processed information in a structured and organized manner. The storage can be physical, such as filing cabinets, or digital, such as databases and servers. The stored information must be secure, accessible, and retrievable to be useful for decision-making.

1. Output

Output is the fourth component of the MIS system. It involves presenting the processed information in a format that is understandable and useful for decision-making. The output can be in different forms, such as reports, graphs, charts, and dashboards. The output must be timely, relevant, and accurate to be useful for decision-making.

1. Feedback

Feedback is the fifth component of the MIS system. It involves providing information on the performance of the MIS system to the stakeholders. Feedback can be in different forms, such as user feedback, system performance reports, and system audit reports. Feedback is essential to improve the performance of the MIS system and make it more effective.

1. Control

Control is the sixth component of the MIS system. It involves monitoring and managing the MIS system to ensure that it meets the requirements of the organization. Control can be manual or automated, depending on the complexity of the system and the requirements of the organization. Control involves various activities, such as system maintenance, system upgrades, and system security.

In conclusion, a Model of MIS System is a comprehensive framework that represents the various components and processes involved in a Management Information System. The model provides a structured approach to collecting, processing, and transforming data into useful information, which is used for decision-making in an organization. The success of the MIS system depends on the accuracy, timeliness, relevance, and accessibility of the information, as well as the efficiency and effectiveness of the system.

Sure, here is an example of a simple snake game made with the ezsgame library:
```python
from ezsgame import *
import random

# Initialize the window
window = Window(title="Snake Game - (Code Sample)", show_fps=True)

# Initialize the snake
snake = Rect(
    pos=Pos("center", "center"),
    size=(20, 20),
    color="white"
)

# Initialize the food
food = Circle(
    pos=Pos(random.randint(0, window.size.width), random.randint(0, window.size.height)),
    radius=5,
    color="red"
)

# Initialize the snake's direction
direction = "right"

# Initialize the snake's tail
tail = []

# Main game loop
while True:
    window.check_events()

    # Clear the screen
    window.fill("black")

    # Update the snake's position based on its direction
    if direction == "right":
        snake.pos.x += snake.size.width
    elif direction == "left":
        snake.pos.x -= snake.size.width
    elif direction == "up":
        snake.pos.y -= snake.size.height
    elif direction == "down":
        snake.pos.y += snake.size.height

    # Add the snake's current position to its tail
    tail.append(snake.pos.copy())
    if len(tail) > 5:
        tail.pop(0)

    # Check if the snake has collided with the food
    if snake.colliderect(food):
        food.pos = Pos(random.randint(0, window.size.width), random.randint(0, window.size.height))
        tail.append(snake.pos.copy())

    # Draw the food
    food.draw()

    # Draw the snake's tail
    for i in range(len(tail)):
        rect = Rect(
            pos=tail[i],
            size=(20, 20),
            color="white"
        )
        rect.draw()

    # Draw the snake
    snake.draw()

    # Update the window
    window.update()
```
In this example, we use the `Rect` and `Circle` classes to create the snake and food objects respectively. We use the `Pos` class to position the snake and food on the screen. We also use a while loop to run the game, and check for user inputs to change the direction of the snake. The snake is also has a tail that is represented by a list of rectangles, and the snake eats the food which generates a new food in random location on the screen.

The key risks and challenges facing "We are all rare" include effectively executing the proposed services and strategies and adapting to market changes. Additionally, the success of "We are all rare" will depend on the ability to attract and retain patients, healthcare professionals, and life science companies. However, given the significant unmet needs in the rare disease community and the growing demand for digital health solutions, there is a strong case for the proposed NGO's potential success.

Conclusion
This executive summary presents a compelling case for the creation of "We are all rare” and it's potential to improve patients' lives, healthcare professionals, life science companies, and health systems. The organization's unique combination of services and strategies, coupled with a dedicated and experienced management team, positions "We are all rare" for success. We look forward to the opportunity to bring this vital work to fruition.



NGO - “We are all Rare"
Ownership
We are all Rare is a non-profit organization whose mission is to improve the quality of life for children and adults with rare diseases. The organization will be incorporated in the United States of America, Florida, as a Limited Liability Company (LLC).

Legal Structure
The LLC structure allows the organization to have a separate legal identity from its owners, protecting them from personal liability for its debts or liabilities. The organization's operating agreement will outline the governance and operations of the NGO, including charitable interests, income from services, grants, charity, and donations, corporate governance, and tax issues. The operating agreement will be registered with the Florida Department of State, Division of Corporations, and publicly available on the NGO's website.

Ownership
As an NGO, We are all Rare will be owned by Tudo Ben Tudo Bom Global and Valeria Bustillos, with Valeria holding 51% of the ownership and TBTB Global holding 49%. All profits or financial proceeds from the organization's operations will be re-invested in the organization to support its mission of improving the quality of life for children living with rare diseases.

Board of Directors and Management Team
The current members of the board of directors and management team include Valeria Bustillos as NGO President, Oscar Bustillos as NGO Development Director, Rodrigo Marcano as NGO Operations Director, Mariela Arevalo as Administrative and Finance Director, Jeison Salamanca as NGO Technology Development Manager, Andrea Villalba as NGO Solutions Architect Director Manager, Andrea Wenzel as NGO Administrative and Financial Manager, Elimar Gonzalez as NGO Operational Manager, Juan David Mesa as NGO Legal Advisor, Tomas Requena as NGO Technology Advisor, and Juan C Castro as NGO Data Science Advisor. The board is responsible for the NGO's strategy, direction, and development. At the same time, the managerial team is responsible for the NGO's operations, and the advisor team is accountable for providing business support to the NGO. The decision-making process is done through bi-weekly board meetings with OKRs analysis and per meeting minute development to trickle down strategy and activities to the Director, Managerial, and advisor team.

Funding Sources
The primary funding sources for the NGO are TBTB Global as the NGO's startup funding and multinational life science companies (current TBTB Global's clients) such as Sanofi, Roche, AstraZeneca, J&J, Alexion, and Novartis, who will fund specific projects development of the NGO. TBTB Global currently finances the NGO, and it is developing partnerships and collaborations with rare disease organizations and life science companies.

Charitable Status
The NGO currently does not possess any tax-exempt status, but it is interested in developing one. Two options being analyzed by the NGO are 501(c)(3) status and 509(a)(2) status. Both options give the NGO tax-exempt status and allow donors to make tax-deductible contributions. However, each option has different requirements and regulations; the NGO is developing its analysis of these options.

Compliance with state and federal regulations
As a Florida LLC, the NGO must comply with state and federal regulations for non-profit organizations. This includes but is not limited to registering with the Florida Department of State, Division of Corporations, and obtaining necessary licenses and permits. It also provides compliance with tax laws and regulations, such as filing annual tax returns with the IRS and the state of Florida.

Transparency, accountability, and governance policies
The NGO ensures transparency and accountability by regularly publishing financial reports and auditing its books and records. Additionally, the NGO has policies to comply with all relevant laws and regulations and will have regular audits by an independent third party. The NGO also ensures transparency by providing regular updates to its stakeholders and the public through its website and other communication channels.
History
We are all Rare is a new non-profit organization aiming to improve the quality of life for children and adults with rare diseases. The organization is a spin-off of TBTB Global, providing diagnostic and care services to patients with rare diseases since 2010.

TBTB Global has a long history of advocating for the rare disease community and has developed a deep understanding of the gaps and needs that the community has. The company has assisted healthcare professionals and patients in accelerating the rare disease diagnostic and treatment journey through innovative technological tools and processes.

However, despite the progress made, much work still needs to be done. Over 7,000 identified rare diseases currently exist, but only 350 have approved treatments. Rare diseases affect a small percentage of the population, with an incidence of approximately 2%. This small percentage represents over 450 million people with rare conditions, many of whom endure a poor quality of life and accelerated deterioration until death.

Due to the rarity and complexity of rare disease signs and symptoms, it takes an average of seven years to diagnose a patient with a rare disease correctly. During this time, patients and their families go through multiple complex health and emotional situations that extensively deteriorate their quality of life. Additionally, once correctly diagnosed, patients must develop an additional journey to access expensive treatments, another obstacle that further contributes to the lengthy deterioration of patients’ life quality. It is not until a patient commences treatment that their health and emotional sense can be improved.

For the past ten years, TBTB Global has enabled adequate support for patients and healthcare professionals by developing over 80,000 diagnostic tests for patients with rare disease suspicion and effectively pinpointing around 3% of specific diseases for patients to access the correct treatment. The company has also supported over 4,000 healthcare professionals with disease suspicion education and helped over 1,200 patients in their treatment journey.

Valeria Bustillos, the founder of We are all Rare, has a passion for helping people, specifically children, be happier in this world. She believes that her love, combined with TBTB Global's expertise, can genuinely improve the quality of life for the rare disease community. The organization will focus on building a more holistic approach to rare disease care, emphasizing improving the quality of life for rare disease patients. This includes addressing the emotional and spiritual needs of patients, as well as their physical health.

The organization will work closely with healthcare professionals, public and private institutions, and the general community to raise awareness about rare diseases and improve patients' diagnostic and treatment journey. We are all Rare will also develop initiatives that improve rare disease patients’ quality of life, focusing on their health and emotional and spiritual cardinals.

In summary, We are all Rare is a new non-profit organization aiming to improve the quality of life for children and adults with rare diseases. The organization is a spin-off of TBTB Global, providing diagnostic and care services to patients with rare diseases since 2010. The organization will focus on building a more holistic approach to rare disease care and will work closely with healthcare professionals, public and private institutions, and the general community to raise awareness about rare diseases and improve patients' diagnostic and treatment journey.
Location
The Head Quarters of “We are all rare” are located in Coal Gables, Miami, Florida, United States. And initial hub offices are located in Santa Maria Business District, Panama City / Panama.

Coordination and Communication
To ensure effective coordination and communication between the multiple offices, "We are all Rare" has established a centralized management structure. This structure will be responsible for managing the organization's resources and ensuring that the goals and objectives of the organization are met. Additionally, the organization has implemented a robust communication system, including regular teleconferencing and video conferencing, to facilitate communication and coordination between the different offices.

 thus have multiple purposes. The more attuned a student is to why they’re being tasked with certain assignments, the more their writing will matter, both for them and others.
 
 It also helps if instructors make it clear to students why they’re given certain tasks. College essays stretch a student to practice certain techniques that do transfer, but students rightly become frustrated when this contextual information is not obvious.
 
 III. Audience: Who will help respond to the exigency?
 As with the section on purpose above, audience has a straightforward relation to the exigency: it’s the reader, listener, or viewer whom the author expects to help solve the problem.
 
 This sensitivity to an audience should help students better appreciate why writing programs emphasize the importance of writing for readers. Journaling and some creative writing situations might have an audience of just one (you, the writer), but most rhetorical situations expect the writer to craft their message in such a way that it appeals to a clearly defined audience.
 
 Writing with an intended audience in mind is one of the most important. factors a writer will consider. It impacts everything in a writing course, from the assignments themselves to the peer review process. Although there are many different types of audiences, it can help to lump them into three very broad categories:
 
 Formal communities, such as a college course or professional workplace. Artifacts geared towards these audiences often adhere to highly codified stylistic conventions and are at least somewhat “tight” as a culture.
 Informal communities, such as for friends, family, or social media. Artifacts written for these audiences still follow some rules, but they’re less likely to be explicitly stated. The culture is usually “looser” than academia and professional institutions.
 These communities are defined more carefully below.
 
 Writing within formal communities
 
 When students are expected to simulate expertise on a topic and address a specialized community, they’re writing within a “discipline” or “field of study.” Writing artifacts produced within that field tend to sound the same, circulate within a certain hierarchy, and are housed by specific institutions. The uniqueness of each field of study (a type of context) often fosters academic jargon. You may have noticed that peer-reviewed academic articles look very similar and are more difficult to read than popular news articles. That’s because peer-reviewed articles are published from within and for highly specific writing communities who share a similar vocabulary and disciplinary knowledge with tightly controlled conventions. A contextual appreciation of an article’s audience allows a specialist to say much more about certain things (a molecular biologist can explain the shape of a protein fold very precisely) and less about other things (the same biologist doesn’t need to explain what a protein compound is). Community-as-context helps a writer better understand how to communicate with their intended audience. The cost of this specialization, however, is that peer-reviewed articles can often lapse into too much jargon!
 
 Even when students aren’t expected to simulate expertise, professors often ask them to submit writing samples that adhere to certain conventions, such as MLA or APA Style. In this way, higher education fosters a culture that the cultural psychologist Michele Gelfand would categorize as relatively “tight.” The tightness of academic culture impacts stylistic requirements (MLA, APA, etc.) as well as what “appropriate grammar” looks like.
 
 Part of this tightness helps explain common expectation surrounding “standard American English” (SAE). Historically, many U.S. academic institutions have penalized deviations from standard English in order to maintain a particular standard that only began to be heavily codified in America in the early nineteenth-century. As a result, variations such as Black English, Spanglish, or sub-dialects related to Southern American English tend to be seen as sub-standard within this broader cultural context that higher education takes for granted (the chapter “Style and Linguistic Diversity” offers more information about this cultural back-history to standard English). Phrases like “appropriate grammar” should be understood contextually, as belonging to a particular formal culture, even if it’s perceived to be dominant.
 
 Some cultural norms carry over into professional workplace environments, especially formal ones. For example, “clear writing” in the professional world is often conflated with with “Is that written in standard English?”, an expectation that begins in education. On the other hand, MLA or APA formatting are usually not required in many workplace environments, unless it happens to overlap with research or publishing.
 
 Understanding the broader cultural context within which “stylistically appropriate” writing is produced should help a student become even more intentional about the choices they make when writing for the intended audience. It should also lead to more awareness about how to critically evaluate the writing within their community. When participating in peer review, for example, a student who’s aware of the cultural context undergirding requirements such as “appropriate grammar” may adopt a more nuanced tone in their revision feedback.
 
 Writing within informal communities
 
 Depending on a student’s background, the vast quantity of their experience with writing on a daily basis might be for an informal audience, such as a family member, close friends, or significant other. But even very informal writing situations, such as texting a friend, sending an email to a family member, or crafting a social media post place certain expectations on the writer/rhetor. These more informal writing situations may or may not have codes of conduct attached to them. They often appear to emerge spontaneously, as unstated contracts between the parties. Certain unsaid codes of conduct determine what you can and cannot say to a parent vs. a group of friends, for example. However, there tend to be fewer rules governing these situations than the formal writing situations, and they’re usually not articulated anywhere. The rules are almost never explicitly enforced by a governing body or institution. Writers within these situations usually feel spontaneous, natural, and authentic.
 
 The basic distinction between informal and formal writing communities helps explain why college assignments often feel so unnatural and difficult.
 
 IV. Genre
 Genre places additional limits on the rhetor/writer/speaker, beyond those related to the beliefs, concerns, and expectations of the audience.
 
 A genre constraint might be the kind of speech a student is asked to deliver to the class. For example, many COMM 100/101 courses ask students to develop expository and persuasive speeches. When preparing for these assessments, students are often given a set of expectations, including a basic structure, style, and whether certain persuasive appeals are relevant. A student can then adopt a topic of their choice to these default expectations.
 
 This process is not dissimilar from how a maid of honor or best man will google “how to do a maid of honor speech” or “how to do a best man’s speech” before a wedding. There are a well-known set of conventions that people draw from–and, at the same time, cultures and sub-cultures will introduce slight innovations within those recurring forms.
 
 The ability to identify a “persuasive college speech” or a “best man’s speech” is both an act of naming and a form of classification. That’s what genre awareness is: an ability to map an assignment or another rhetorical situation to a relatively stable form that recurs across time and space. A Six-Week Program for Developing Creativity and Imagination:

Week 1: Mindfulness and Meditation

* Introduction to mindfulness and meditation
* Understanding the connection between mindfulness, imagination, and creativity
* Daily mindfulness and meditation practices
* Setting goals and intentions for the course
* Actionable plan: Practice mindfulness and meditation for 15-20 minutes each day, journal your experience and insights

Week 2: Surrounding Yourself with Positive and Supportive Individuals

* Understanding the impact of positive social connections on creativity and imagination
* Finding and connecting with like-minded individuals
* Building supportive relationships that encourage creativity and imagination
* Actionable plan: Attend a social event or join a club or group that aligns with your interests and values, engage in meaningful conversations with others to build positive relationships

Week 3: Engaging in New Experiences and Activities

* Understanding the connection between new experiences and imaginative thinking
* Engaging in experiences that challenge your perspectives and tap into your imagination
* Actionable plan: Engage in one new experience each week that pushes you out of your comfort zone, document your experiences and insights in your journal

Week 4: Journaling and Reflection

* Understanding the role of journaling in developing creativity and imagination
* Reflecting on experiences and insights to unlock your full creative potential
* Actionable plan: Set aside dedicated time each day for journaling, reflect on your experiences and insights, and identify areas for further growth and development

Week 5: Brainstorming and Collaboration

* Understanding the power of collaboration and brainstorming for generating creative ideas
* Engaging in structured brainstorming sessions with others
* Building on the contributions of others to develop innovative solutions
* Actionable plan: Seek out opportunities to engage in structured brainstorming sessions with others, document your brainstorming experiences and insights in your journal

Week 6: Visualization and Positive Self-Talk

* Understanding the impact of visualization and positive self-talk on creativity and imagination
* Cultivating a positive and supportive mindset through visualization and positive self-talk
* Actionable plan: Practice visualization techniques each day, engage in positive self-talk, and reflect on your experiences and insights in your journal.

The Applied Formula for Developing Creativity and Imagination:

C = M + S + E + J + B + V

Where:

* C represents Creativity and Imagination
* M represents Mindfulness and Meditation
* S represents Surrounding Yourself with Positive and Supportive Individuals
* E represents Engaging in New Experiences and Activities
* J represents Journaling and Reflection
* B represents Brainstorming and Collaboration
* V represents Visualization and Positive Self-Talk

In conclusion, developing creativity and imagination requires a holistic approach that incorporates mindfulness and meditation, positive social connections, new experiences and activities, journaling and reflection, structured brainstorming sessions, and visualization and positive self-talk. By incorporating these practices into your daily routine, you can unlock your full creative and imaginative potential and achieve your desired outcomes.

Sure! Here's the updated code with Roberta base model and EarlyStoppingCallback added:
```python
from transformers import RobertaForSequenceClassification, RobertaTokenizerFast
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold
from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import TrainingArguments, Trainer, EarlyStoppingCallback

# Load dataset
dataset = load_dataset("csv", data_files={"train": "./data/train.csv", "validation": "./data/val.csv"}, delimiter=",")

# Load tokenizer and encode the dataset
tokenizer = RobertaTokenizerFast.from_pretrained('roberta-base')
def encode_dataset(d):
    return tokenizer(d['text'], padding='max_length', truncation=True, max_length=512)
dataset = dataset.map(encode_dataset)

# Define training arguments
training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir='./results',          # output directory
    num_train_epochs=3,              # total number of training epochs
    per_device_train_batch_size=32,  # batch size per device during training
    per_device_eval_batch_size=64,   # batch size for evaluation
    warmup_steps=500,                # number of warmup steps for learning rate scheduler
    weight_decay=0.01,               # strength of weight decay
    logging_dir='./logs',            # directory for storing logs
    logging_steps=10,
    evaluation_strategy='epoch',     # evaluation strategy to adopt during training
    save_total_limit=3,              # maximum number of checkpoints to save
    eval_steps=None,                 # number of steps between evaluations
    save_strategy='epoch',           # save checkpoint every eval_steps
    load_best_model_at_end=True,     # load the best model when finished training
    metric_for_best_model='accuracy',# use accuracy to evaluate the best model
    early_stopping_patience=2        # stop training early if no improvement for 2 evaluations
)

# Define k-fold cross-validation
kfold = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

for fold, (train_indices, val_indices) in enumerate(kfold.split(dataset)):
    # Split the dataset into train and validation sets for this fold
    train_dataset = dataset.select(train_indices)
    val_dataset = dataset.select(val_indices)

    # Load model and prepare the Trainer
    model = RobertaForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained('roberta-base', num_labels=8)

    trainer = Trainer(
        model=model,
        args=training_args,
        train_dataset=train_dataset,
        eval_dataset=val_dataset,
        compute_metrics=lambda p: {'accuracy': (p.predictions.argmax(axis=-1) == p.label_ids).mean()},
        callbacks=[EarlyStoppingCallback(early_stopping_patience=2)]  # add EarlyStoppingCallback
    )

    # Train the model for this fold
    trainer.train()
```
Note that the `EarlyStoppingCallback` is added to the `callbacks` argument of the `Trainer` object with `early_stopping_patience` set to 2. This means that the training will be stopped early if the evaluation metric does not improve for 2 consecutive evaluations on the validation set.

The United States is in danger of missing a profound change in the economic component of China’s geopolitical strategy. Chinese President Xi Jinping has downgraded the Communist Party’s ambition to overtake the U.S. in economic size (though that is still officially a goal). Instead, his priority is to minimize China’s dependence on other countries and maximize its ability to coerce them economically. This is an implicit acknowledgment that China can’t achieve the aim of being a truly rich nation anytime soon. But the U.S. cannot afford to be complacent: China can wield its very large economy as a strategic weapon.
Just as the U.S. previously needed to respond to a China that was bent on becoming the world’s foremost economy, Washington now needs to respond to a China bent on long-term economic coercion to secure the interests of the Communist party and the Chinese nation. Domestic action by the U.S. is important, and is easier to achieve, starting with better understanding Xi’s goals. Internationally, to persuade friends and allies to limit their reliance on China, the U.S. must revive a moribund trade policy.
Xi clarified China’s new approach in a series of speeches in 2020, claiming that the “powerful gravitational field” of the state-controlled Chinese market can be used to reshape supply chains in Beijing’s favor. In Xi’s view, this is essential in what he’s called the “great struggle” against Western efforts to limit China’s technological advancement and target its import vulnerabilities.
China has, of course, long engaged in industrial espionage and coercive technology transfer. And Xi’s “Made in China 2025” industrial plan has, since 2015, provided sweeping government assistance to such sectors as semiconductors and electric vehicles. Xi now seems to believe that China must redouble efforts to tilt economic leverage in its favor, as Beijing responds to what it views as an evolving American strategy of containment. Xi may see the decoupling of the two countries’ economies as ultimately inevitable—and may now be actively advancing it, on his preferred terms.
At home, Xi evidently fears that a thriving private sector risks powerful constituencies developing outside party control—he has cracked down on activities perceived as threatening in this respect. With the party determined to retain control of the economy, potentially productive industries face many barriers to expansion. In their place are sectors that serve the party’s interests first. This is not conducive to innovation and scientific breakthrough and, along with deteriorating demographics and high debt, will continue to limit growth.

```python
                    print('Payload released. Target: {}, Location: {}'.format(target, location))
                    break
                    
            if not face_match:
                # unauthorized user detected
                # send alert
                print('Unauthorized user detected. Sending alert.')
                
            # update the last detected face and time
            last_face = face
            last_time = datetime.datetime.now()
    
    # show the frame
    cv2.imshow('frame', frame)
    
    # check for quit command
    if cv2.waitKey(1) & 0xFF == ord('q'):
        break

# cleanup
camera.release()
cv2.destroyAllWindows()
```
This code uses OpenCV for face detection and facial recognition. It also uses datetime for timing and logging. The code checks if the detected face is authorized and if so, releases the payload and logs the activity. If an unauthorized user is detected, an alert is sent. The code also shows the camera feed in a window and can be quit by pressing 'q'.

In Linux, you can find and kill the process using a specific port with the following steps:

1. Open a terminal window.
2. Run the following command to find the process ID (PID) of the process using the port:
```bash
sudo lsof -i :<port>
```
Replace `<port>` with the port number you want to check. For example, to find the process using port 8080, the command would be:
```bash
sudo lsof -i :8080
```
1. Look for the line showing the process name and the PID. The PID will be in the second column.
2. Use the `kill` command to terminate the process by its PID:
```bash
sudo kill <PID>
```
Replace `<PID>` with the process ID you found in step 3.

For example, if the PID is 1234, the command would be:
```bash
sudo kill 1234
```
If the process doesn't terminate with the regular `kill` command, you can forcefully terminate it using the `kill -9` command:
```bash
sudo kill -9 <PID>
```
This will forcefully terminate the process,

There are several statistical tests to test for autocorrelation and heteroscedasticity in time series data. Here are some common tests and how to perform them in R:

1. Autocorrelation test:
The most common statistical test for autocorrelation is the Ljung-Box test, which tests the null hypothesis that there is no autocorrelation in the time series data up to a specified lag. The test calculates a test statistic and p-value, where a p-value less than the significance level indicates that the null hypothesis can be rejected in favor of the alternative hypothesis of autocorrelation.

In R, you can perform the Ljung-Box test using the Box.test() function. Here is an example:
```r
# Load the AirPassengers dataset and convert it to a time series object
data(AirPassengers)
tsdata <- ts(AirPassengers, start = c(1949, 1), end = c(1960, 12), frequency = 12)

# Perform the Ljung-Box test for autocorrelation up to lag 12
Box.test(tsdata, lag = 12, type = "Ljung-Box")
```
This code performs the Ljung-Box test for autocorrelation up to lag 12 on the AirPassengers dataset.

1. Heteroscedasticity test:
One of the most common statistical tests for heteroscedasticity is the Breusch-Pagan test, which tests the null hypothesis that the variance of the residuals in a regression model is constant. The test calculates a test statistic and p-value, where a p-value less than the significance level indicates that the null hypothesis can be rejected in favor of the alternative hypothesis of heteroscedasticity.

In R, you can perform the Breusch-Pagan test using the bptest() function from the lmtest package. Here is an example:
```r
# Fit a linear regression model to the AirPassengers dataset
fit <- lm(tsdata ~ time(tsdata))

# Perform the Breusch-Pagan test for heteroscedasticity
library(lmtest)
bptest(fit)
```
This code fits a linear regression model to the AirPassengers dataset and performs the Breusch-Pagan test for heteroscedasticity on the residuals of the model.

Here are the steps you can follow when creating a pillar page:

1. Choose a broad topic: Select a topic that is relevant to your business and can cover multiple subtopics. The topic should be broad enough to be broken down into subtopics but narrow enough to stay focused on a specific area.
2. Identify subtopics: Identify subtopics related to your broad topic and create a list. These subtopics should cover all aspects of the broad topic, and each subtopic should be able to stand on its own as a blog post.
3. Create a structure: Organize the subtopics in a logical order, creating a structure for your pillar page. You may choose to start with an introduction and overview of the broad topic, followed by the subtopics in a logical order, ending with a conclusion.
4. Write high-quality content: Create high-quality content for each subtopic. Ensure that each section is informative, engaging, and provides value to the reader. Use images, infographics, and other media to enhance the content.
5. Optimize the content: Optimize your content for search engines by using relevant keywords, meta descriptions, and optimizing images. This will help your pillar page rank higher in search engine results pages (SERPs).
6. Link to relevant content: Link to relevant blog posts, articles, or other resources within your website. This will provide additional information to the reader and keep them engaged with your website.
7. Promote your pillar page: Promote your pillar page on social media, email newsletters, and other marketing channels. This will help drive traffic to your website and increase engagement with your content.
8. Update your pillar page: Update your pillar page periodically with new content, statistics, and other relevant information. This will keep your pillar page relevant and fresh, providing ongoing value to your readers.

There is a general consensus among scholars that deaconesses played a significant role in the early church, particularly in ministering to women. This included assisting with the baptism of women, especially in situations where the baptism would require full immersion, which was traditionally performed in the nude. Deaconesses were also involved in teaching female catechumens and helping to care for the sick and needy.

However, there is less consensus regarding the extent of the authority and responsibility of deaconesses. Some scholars argue that they held a similar level of authority as male deacons, while others suggest that their role was more limited and focused on serving women. There is also debate about whether deaconesses were ordained or simply appointed to their role, as well as whether their role was permanent or temporary. Overall, the evidence is somewhat limited, and different interpretations of the available sources have led to varying views on the role and function of deaconesses in the early church.

```python
# Import necessary libraries

def analyze_crop(image_path):
    # Load image from file
    img = cv2.imread(image_path)
    
    # Apply image processing techniques to enhance features
    img = cv2.resize(img, (224, 224))
    img = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB)
    img = np.expand_dims(img, axis=0)
    img = img / 255.0

    model = load_model('crop_ripeness_model.h5')

    ripeness_score = predictions[0][0]
    
    return ripeness_score
```

Introduction:
Welcome to week 4 of the course, where we dive deeper into the power of visualization. This week, we'll explore how visualization can help you tap into your creative potential and manifest your deepest desires. You'll learn about the science behind visualization, as well as techniques for effective visualization.

Main Course Content:

Understanding Visualization:
Visualization is a powerful tool that can help you bring your goals and aspirations to life. By visualizing what you want, you create a vivid mental image of what you want to achieve, making it easier to bring your vision to life.

The Science Behind Visualization:
Visualization is rooted in the power of the mind. When we visualize, our minds send signals to our bodies that trigger the release of certain hormones and neurotransmitters. These hormones and neurotransmitters help to activate our subconscious mind and bring our desires to life.

Techniques for Effective Visualization:
There are many techniques for effective visualization, including guided visualization, visualization journaling, and visualization meditation. Each technique has its own unique benefits and can help you tap into the power of visualization in a way that works best for you.

Real-life Examples of Visualization in Action:
There are countless examples of individuals who have used visualization to achieve their goals and aspirations. For instance, Olympic athletes often use visualization techniques to help them perform at their best. Visualization has also been used to help individuals overcome obstacles, such as illness or injury, and to achieve financial success.

Self-Prompting Questions:

* What are my goals and aspirations?
* How can visualization help me achieve these goals?
* What visualization techniques work best for me?

Application Formula:

1. Set clear, achievable goals and aspirations.
2. Choose a visualization technique that works best for you.
3. Visualize your goals and aspirations regularly.
4. Use positive affirmations to reinforce your visualization.
5. Take action towards your goals and aspirations.
6. Celebrate your progress and continue to refine and refine your visualization technique.

By using visualization, you'll be able to tap into the power of your mind and bring your desires to life. Remember to be patient, persistent, and stay positive. The more you practice visualization, the better you'll become at manifesting your deepest desires.

Sure, here's a modified version of the script that uses Selenium to scrape the search results:
```python
from selenium import webdriver
from selenium.webdriver.common.keys import Keys
from datetime import datetime
import time

# Define the keywords to search for
keywords = ["live events", "virtual events", "conferences", "meetings"]

# Define the URL you want to scrape
url = "https://www.google.com/"

# Define the path to the web driver executable
driver_path = "/path/to/chromedriver"

# Create a new instance of the Chrome driver
driver = webdriver.Chrome(driver_path)

# Navigate to the Google search page
driver.get(url)

# Find the search box and enter the keywords
search_box = driver.find_element_by_name("q")
search_box.send_keys(" ".join(keywords))
search_box.send_keys(Keys.RETURN)

# Wait for the search results to load
time.sleep(5)

# Define a list to store the URLs of the websites
urls = []

# Find all the relevant search result divs on the page
search_results = driver.find_elements_by_css_selector('div.g')

# Loop through the search results and extract the URLs
for result in search_results:
    link = result.find_element_by_css_selector('a')
    url = link.get_attribute("href")
    if url.startswith("http"):
        urls.append(url)

# Define the output filename with a timestamp
output_filename = "EventScraperOutput_" + datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S") + ".txt"

# Write the URLs to the output file
with open(output_filename, "w") as f:
    for url in urls:
        f.write(url + "\n")

# Close the browser
driver.close()

# Print the number of URLs found and the output filename
print(f"Found {len(urls)} URLs. Output written to {output_filename}.")
```
In this modified script, we import the `webdriver` module from Selenium and create a new instance of the Chrome driver. We then navigate to the Google search page and enter the keywords in the search box using the `find_element_by_name()` and `send_keys()` methods.

After waiting for the search results to load, we find all the relevant search result divs on the page using the `find_elements_by_css_selector()` method, and loop through them to extract the URLs. We use the `get_attribute()` method to retrieve the value of the `href` attribute of the link element.

Finally, we write the URLs to a file, close the browser, and print the number of URLs found and the output filename.

Note that you will need to download and install the Chrome driver executable and specify its path in the `driver_path` variable for this script to work. You can download the Chrome driver from the following link: <https://sites.google.com/a/chromium.org/chromedriver/downloads>

Sure! Here's the code that you asked for, which filters a DataFrame based on a range condition applied to multiple columns and creates a new column that shows which columns are outside of the range:
```python
import pandas as pd

# Sample data
data = {'column_1': [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
        'column_2': [6, 7, 8, 9, 10],
        'column_3': [11, 12, 13, 14, 15]}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Filter condition
condition = (df > 2) & (df < 5)

# Create a new column 'out_of_range' to store the result of the filter
df['out_of_range'] = condition.apply(lambda row: ', '.join(df.columns[row]), axis=1)
df['out_of_range'] = df['out_of_range'].where(condition.any(1), '')

print(df)
```
This code creates a new column `out_of_range` in the DataFrame to store the result of the filter. The `condition.apply` method is used to apply the filter condition to each row in the DataFrame, and the `lambda` function is used to convert each row of the `condition` DataFrame into a string of comma-separated column names. The `axis=1` argument is used to apply the `lambda` function to each row. The `condition.any(1)` method is used to find the rows that meet the filter condition, and the `df['out_of_range'].where` method is used to replace the values of the `out_of_range` column with an empty string for the rows that don't meet the filter condition.

Sure, here's an example of a complete base repository pattern with Pydantic models and an example of API endpoints using the FastAPI framework:

First, we'll define a Pydantic model for a user:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from bson import ObjectId

class User(BaseModel):
    id: str = None
    name: str
    email: str
    password: str

    class Config:
        allow_population_by_field_name = True
        arbitrary_types_allowed = True
        json_encoders = {
            ObjectId: str
        }
```
In this example, the `User` Pydantic model represents a user, with properties for the user's id, name, email, and password.

Next, we'll define a base repository class:
```python
from typing import List
from pymongo.collection import Collection
from bson import ObjectId

class BaseRepository:
    def __init__(self, collection: Collection):
        self.collection = collection

    def find_all(self) -> List[User]:
        result = []
        for user in self.collection.find():
            result.append(User(**user))
        return result

    def find_by_id(self, id: str) -> User:
        user = self.collection.find_one({'_id': ObjectId(id)})
        if user:
            return User(**user)
        else:
            return None

    def save(self, user: User) -> User:
        user_dict = user.dict(exclude_unset=True)
        result = self.collection.insert_one(user_dict)
        user.id = str(result.inserted_id)
        return user

    def update(self, id: str, user: User) -> User:
        user_dict = user.dict(exclude_unset=True)
        result = self.collection.update_one(
            {'_id': ObjectId(id)},
            {'$set': user_dict},
            upsert=False
        )
        if result.modified_count == 1:
            return user
        else:
            return None

    def delete(self, id: str) -> User:
        user = self.find_by_id(id)
        if user:
            self.collection.delete_one({'_id': ObjectId(id)})
            return user
        else:
            return None
```
In this example, the `BaseRepository` class provides a base implementation for interacting with the database, using the `pymongo` library. It provides methods for finding, creating, updating, and deleting users.

The `find_all` method returns a list of all `User` objects in the database. The `find_by_id` method takes an `id` and returns the `User` object with that `id` from the database.

The `save` method takes a `User` object, creates a new `User` object with a generated `id`, and saves it to the database. It returns the newly created `User` object.

The `update` method takes an `id` and a `User` object with the updated values, and updates the `User` object in the database with the new values. It returns the updated `User` object.

The `delete` method takes an `id` and deletes the corresponding `User` object from the database. It returns the deleted `User` object.

Next, we can define a concrete repository class that extends the `BaseRepository`:
```python
class UserRepository(BaseRepository):
    def __init__(self, collection: Collection):
        super().__init__(collection)
```
In this example, the `UserRepository` class extends the `BaseRepository` class, and provides a concrete implementation of the repository for the `User` model.

Finally, we can use the `UserRepository` class to create an API endpoint:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
```

I will provide another piece of content to be used as an input about Rolling Loud and Loudpunx below:

Rolling Loud and LoudPunx
Earlier this morning, I came across a large Twitter Spaces hosted by LoudPunx, the NFT project from Rolling Loud. Even Gary Vee (3.1M followers on Twitter) popped in to pledge his support for the project as he is friends with the founder Tariq.

Side note about Gary Vee’s appearance
When Gary Vee joined the Twitter Spaces, the listener count went from ~900 to ~1500.

One masterful thing that Gary Vee did was he called out the names of his Veefriends holders that followed him into the Twitter Spaces, as indicated by the listener’s PFP. In total he probably called out 20+ of his community members that were listening in while verbally expressing his support for LoudPunx.

Why is this notable?

Gary is expressing his gratitude for all of his supporters, regardless of where he is. Additionally, he didn’t do it in a way that was disruptive to the flow of conversation that was focused on the LoudPunx launch. You can tell that Gary is dedicated to his community and is serious about providing value to them, even if it’s a shoutout.

Founders take note, how can you thoughtfully show gratitude to your community? It doesn’t have to be in the form of another NFT airdrop. A shoutout (or retweeting a community member’s content — see what BAYC does) provides value that a floor price increase doesn’t.

Back to Rolling Loud
What’s Rolling Loud? It’s a music festival that started in 2015 and has established itself as one of the premier festivals for hip hop fans. For anyone into hip hop, a lineup like this is massive.
Source
LoudPunx is priced at a hefty 1.5 ETH (~$2,500) with 5,555 supply. This is steep price, but what does the price tag entail?

Lifetime VIP access to all future Rolling Loud festivals in California, Miami, NYC, Toronto, Portugal, Germany, Netherlands, Thailand and more.

Access to an exclusive LoudPunx holders area within the VIP areas

Giveaways to associated acts and their events (eg: There will be a holders-only raffle for Lil Wayne tour tickets)

Exclusive insight to Rolling Loud content and future projects

Access to premium merchandise drops and collaboration

The first point is what gets interesting. This is a lifetime pass. How much does a VIP pass cost at a Rolling Loud event?
If I were a Rolling Loud superfan and purchased this NFT, the LoudPunx NFT would pay itself back by the third event and would be an absolute no-brainer. On top of that, Rolling Loud isn’t just a once a year event — There are 8 scheduled festivals in various locations around the world in 2023 alone.

So it’s clear from a value perspective that a LoudPunx NFT is a steal. However, the reality of the situation is that:

This is a web3 specific effort so the addressable audience is smaller than Rolling Loud’s total audience

The concept of ‘time to value’ warps the consumer’s perception of this opportunity

1.5 ETH/$2,500 upfront is steep. No payment plans or BNPL

5,555 supply is a lot considering the sticker price

When thinking of ‘time to value’, I think back to my 24 Hour Fitness gym membership when I first graduated college.
The commoner’s gym, it was great!
If I recall correctly, I paid $59 per month for Super Sport access. I knew that Costco sold a 2-year All-Club Super Sport membership for $700 flat. If I purchased the 2-year membership, I could have achieved ‘breakeven’ value in a year.

No-brainer right?!

Well, for some silly reason, TPan never took advantage of the obvious Costco deal despite knowing that it was worth it. Thinking back on the indecision, one of the reasons was I didn’t know if I would end up moving (unreasonable since this was all-club access). The other reason was I didn’t want to pay $700 upfront regardless of the value.

The same could happen with LoudPunx, but that’s where the beauty of web3 and tokenization of the NFT provides liquidity for those that want in or out on the lifetime membership.

With the LoudPunx launch, we’re starting to see a larger sample size of live-event collections testing out a pass model.

Note: This is not comprehensive, but illustrative to the different models that are being rolled out.
Some notes on the other comps:

The Carton: The Lyrical Lemonade team decided on a 3 years of access which shows perpetual utility for a longer length of time, not forever.

The Tick3t: This effort was not built off an existing owned & operated business model. The Tick3t essentially pools together capital from the memberships to season tickets to venues around the world. They incorporate a waiver (waitlist) system to holders. When holder redeem their NFT for access to an event, they move to the bottom of the waiver.

Below is an example of their upcoming calendar, and the past events calendar is here.
Source
As enticing as the seats and events are, the primary disadvantage of a this model is that the company doesn’t manage the events themselves. This limits the capacity for each event and results in the reliance of a waiver system for ticket redemption.

The value for a holder can still be worth it, but is dependent how the team strategically allocates funds to purchasing seats and season tickets.

It seems the that community hasn’t seen the value they expected as the price for these passes has dipped down to .09 ETH (~$150). Additionally, the waiver system may encourage selling the pass once it has been redeemed, adding to the volatility in the secondary market.

Superf3st: The Superf3st Superpass provides free access to the 2023 Superf3st and allows for active participation for the direction of the event. 1 Pass = 1 Vote.

Putting it all Together
As mentioned earlier, the ‘right’ model around NFTs functioning as an access pass to IRL events is still unclear. As more event pass NFT program rise, we’ll begin to identify which ones work, and which ones don’t.

I would also consider the following themes:

What’s the business goal?

I have to assume that the Rolling Loud team understands that LoudPunx functioning as a lifetime pass is a losing proposition from a ROI standpoint. I think they’re betting on LoudPunx being the VIP of VIP status for all their events. Status matters.

Because LoudPunx holders get treated as royalty at physical events, other attendees will notice and want in.

It’s important to note that Rolling Loud can probably eat the cost if the effort is unsuccessful. They’ve held festivals with over 200,000 attendees. Lifetime passes or NFTs with evergreen are a long-term bet, not an ephemeral one.

When viewing this effort from a longer term perspective, an effort like LoudPunx becomes advantageous. LoudPunx allows Rolling Loud to roll out web3 enablement in a thoughtful manner and test out integrations with a smaller audience before large-scale deployment. LoudPunx holders will also be more familiar with using web3-enabled tech, so they’d be perfect for QA vs. a clueless user.

If successful, what would Rolling Loud consider?

Creating lifetime GA passes and/or test different structures by length (eg: 3-year pass) or region (Asia pass)

Rolling out tech integrations to all events

NFT ticketing with partners like Tokenproof

Paying for food/drinks via tokens. What if Rolling Loud created their own digital currency based on brand engagement and loyalty similar to the Cool Score mentioned earlier?

Rolling Loud specific marketplaces for ticket purchase and sale and take a cut of secondary sales

Perks vs. Participation

In the coming months and years in the there will be more distinction between perks and participation.

One of the themes of web3 consumer brands is that there is a deeper relationship between the community member and the company/team. However, the reality is not everyone wants that. Most people want the front row seat to the sporting match. They don’t want to be the coach determining the plays.

Data

As always, there’s a data play 😉

With LoudPunx, the Rolling Loud team can add another dimension to understanding their attendees, especially if they work with a web3 ticketing partner.

With a large enough of a sample size, I’d want to understand:

Are LoudPunx holders previous attendees? How many events did they attend before vs. after? Did they attend international events before vs after?

How often are passes being redeemed at Rolling Loud events?

What are the spending habits of holders ex-tickets before vs. after? If they’re spending more because they don’t have to pay for tickets, where are they spending it?

How would these insights impact attendee behavior if this effort rolled out to a larger subset of the Rolling Loud audience?

On a related note, if Rolling Loud were to expand their web3 onboarding efforts, they could airdrop every attendee a free food ticket, while also understanding food vendor preferences.

On another related note (uh oh, TPan is going off the rails), Rolling Loud could airdrop specific promotions with sponsors or event participants:

Get 50% off if you buy a Bud Light

Get 20% off Travis Scott merch on the day he headlines

If you arrive before 2pm, get 25% off food

Live events are going to get interesting with web3. I can’t wait to attend my first web3 enabled music festival if my old creaky bones cooperate 👴🏻

Each objective has co-leaders – deans and vice provosts with responsibilities tied to the objective – who will guide our efforts, involve stakeholders and lead implementation of strategies and action steps and help ensure continuous progress and success. Student Success: 

Increase Enrollment: We aim to expand our diverse community of ambitious and talented students, increasing access to a world class education for more first time freshman, new transfers, returning and graduate students. HEALTHY & VIBRANT COMMUNITIES 

Strengthen Service to Local and Global Communities: Strengthening our service to local and global communities provides opportunities for our talented staff, faculty, and students to impact expansive programs near and far from KU. The volume and quality of services provided reflects our aspiration to impact communities across the world. 

Improve Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Belonging: With the ever-changing landscape of our country, and world, it is important to the University of Kansas that we make changes that create an environment of fairness, respect, and belonging. All students, staff, and faculty should have the greatest opportunities to meet their academic and professional goals. KU will examine and expand programs in order to meet our aspirations. 

Improve Health and Wellness: There is more to academic success than grades and graduation. At the University of Kansas, we want all our Jayhawks to have the highest quality of life. HttpAdapters in Spring Boot are used to provide a consistent way of interacting with HTTP endpoints, such as RESTful web services. Here are some best practices for using HttpAdapters in Spring Boot:

1. Use interfaces to define HttpAdapter implementations: Define an interface that defines the methods for each HTTP endpoint you want to interact with, and then provide one or more implementations of the interface using HttpAdapters. This approach allows you to easily switch between different implementations, such as different versions of an API, or different implementations for different environments.
2. Use HttpAdapterBuilder to configure HttpAdapters: Spring Boot provides an HttpAdapterBuilder class that can be used to configure HttpAdapters. Use HttpAdapterBuilder to set default headers, configure connection pools, or configure retry policies.
3. Use a central place to manage HttpAdapter instances: Rather than creating HttpAdapter instances manually, consider creating them using a factory or builder pattern in a central place in your application. This makes it easier to manage HttpAdapter instances, such as configuring them with default headers, and allows you to easily switch between different implementations.
4. Keep HttpAdapter interfaces simple and focused: Use small, focused interfaces for HttpAdapter implementations that are easy to understand and use. Avoid overloading interfaces with too many methods or unnecessary complexity.
5. Use HttpAdapter tests to verify API contracts: When using HttpAdapters to interact with external APIs, use tests to verify that the HttpAdapter implementations are correctly implementing the API contracts. This includes verifying expected HTTP status codes, headers, and response body formats.
6. Keep HttpAdapter configuration separate from business logic: Avoid hardcoding HttpAdapter configuration values in business logic. Instead, use properties files or environment variables to configure HttpAdapter instances. This makes it easier to switch between different implementations and configurations, without affecting business logic.

By following these best practices, you can use HttpAdapters effectively in your Spring Boot application, providing a consistent and reliable way of interacting with HTTP endpoints.

Sure! Here are some general UI text writing guidelines and rules to keep in mind:

1. Use Clear and Concise Language: UI text should be written using clear and concise language that is easy for users to understand. Avoid using technical jargon or overly complex terms that may confuse or frustrate users.
2. Be Action-Oriented: UI text should be action-oriented, prompting users to take specific actions or providing guidance on how to proceed. Use active verbs and avoid using passive voice or ambiguous language.
3. Be Consistent: Consistency is key in UI writing to ensure that the language used is clear and familiar to users. Use consistent terminology and formatting throughout the interface, and follow established conventions for labels and buttons.
4. Consider the Context: UI text should be written with the context in mind. Consider where the text will appear and what the user is trying to accomplish. Use language that is relevant to the user and the task at hand.
5. Provide Feedback: UI text should provide feedback to the user, letting them know that their action has been completed or that there is an error that needs to be fixed. Use language that is clear and provides guidance on how to proceed.
6. Use Visual Cues: Use visual cues, such as icons and images, to help users understand the purpose and function of UI elements. Use these cues consistently throughout the interface to provide a clear and intuitive user experience.
7. Prioritize Information: Prioritize the most important information in UI text, placing it in a prominent location or using bold text to make it stand out. Use progressive disclosure to reveal additional information as needed.
8. Use Proper Grammar and Spelling: UI text should be written using proper grammar and spelling to ensure that it is clear and professional. Use a spellchecker and proofread your text carefully to avoid errors.

By following these guidelines and rules, you can create effective and user-friendly UI text that enhances the user experience and helps users to navigate the interface more easily.

Here's an example of Python code that could be used for crop yield prediction using GIS satellite data and machine learning:
```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

# Load historical crop data
crop_data = pd.read_csv('crop_data.csv')

# Load satellite imagery data
satellite_data = pd.read_csv('satellite_data.csv')

# Merge the two datasets on location and date
combined_data = pd.merge(crop_data, satellite_data, on=['location', 'date'])

# Split the data into training and testing sets
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(combined_data.drop(['yield'], axis=1), combined_data['yield'], test_size=0.2, random_state=42)

# Fit a linear regression model to the training data
model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Predict crop yields for the testing data
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)

# Calculate mean squared error
mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred)

# Print the mean squared error
print("Mean squared error:", mse)
```
This code loads historical crop data and satellite imagery data, merges the two datasets on location and date, and splits the data into training and testing sets. It then fits a linear regression model to the training data, predicts crop yields for the testing data, and calculates the mean squared error. This is just one example of how GIS satellite data and machine learning can be used for crop yield prediction - there are many other algorithms and techniques that could be used depending on the specific needs and requirements of the project.

Sure, here are some additional ideas and activities that you can use to teach colors to young children:

1. Color scavenger hunt: Give children a list of colors and ask them to find objects in the room that match each color. This can be a fun and interactive way to reinforce color recognition skills.
2. Color sorting game: Provide children with a variety of objects in different colors and ask them to sort them into groups based on color. This can help improve their ability to distinguish between different colors.
3. Color mixing activity: Provide children with different colors of paint or colored water and ask them to mix them together to create new colors. This can help them understand how primary colors combine to create secondary colors.
4. Color collage: Provide children with a variety of colored materials such as construction paper, tissue paper, and stickers, and ask them to create a collage using different colors. This can help improve their fine motor skills and creativity.
5. Color word search: Create a word search puzzle using color words and ask children to find all the words. This can help improve their spelling and reinforce color word recognition.
6. Color dance party: Play music and ask children to dance using different colored scarves or ribbons. When the music stops, they must freeze and hold up the color of the scarf or ribbon they are holding. This can be a fun and active way to reinforce color recognition skills.
7. Colorful snacks: Provide children with a variety of colorful fruits and vegetables and ask them to sort them into groups based on color. This can help them understand the connection between colors and different types of food.

These are just a few ideas for teaching colors to young children. You can modify or adapt these activities based on the children's age, abilities, and interests.

3.1. Machine Learning and Deep Learning in Retrosynthetic Analysis

In recent years, machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) techniques have emerged as powerful tools to address the limitations of manual retrosynthetic analysis and traditional computational methods. These data-driven approaches can learn complex patterns and relationships from large chemical datasets, enabling more accurate and efficient predictions of retrosynthetic routes (86, 87). This section discusses the application of ML and DL in retrosynthetic analysis, highlighting key advancements and methodologies.

1. Neural network models: Neural networks, particularly deep learning models like convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and recurrent neural networks (RNNs), have been applied to predict retrosynthetic routes by learning complex representations of molecular structures and reaction patterns (88, 89). For example, the work by Segler et al. (90) employed a combination of CNNs and RNNs to predict suitable precursors for a given target molecule.
2. Sequence-to-sequence models: Sequence-to-sequence models, which are typically based on RNNs and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks, have been applied to translate molecular representations of target compounds into precursor molecules, mimicking the process of retrosynthetic disconnection (91, 92). The work by Liu et al. (93) used a sequence-to-sequence model to predict high-level disconnection patterns for a given target molecule.
3. Graph convolutional networks (GCNs): GCNs have been used to model molecular graphs for predicting retrosynthetic routes, taking advantage of their ability to capture local and global structural features of molecules (94, 95). For instance, Coley et al. (96) developed a graph-convolutional approach to predict retrosynthetic routes by learning a continuous representation of molecular structures and reaction templates.
4. Reinforcement learning (RL): RL techniques, such as Q-learning and deep Q-networks (DQNs), have been applied to retrosynthetic analysis by modeling the disconnection process as a decision-making problem, where the algorithm iteratively learns the optimal actions to perform at each step (97, 98). The work by Zhou et al. (99) employed a DQN-based approach to navigate the retrosynthetic tree and identify efficient synthetic routes.
5. Transfer learning: Transfer learning techniques have been used to leverage pre-trained models and knowledge from related tasks to improve the performance of retrosynthetic analysis models, particularly when training data is limited (100, 101). The work by Schwaller et al. (102) demonstrated the effectiveness of transfer learning in predicting reaction templates for retrosynthetic disconnections.
6. Multi-task learning: Multi-task learning methods, which involve training a single model to perform multiple tasks simultaneously, have been used to improve the accuracy and generalizability of retrosynthetic analysis models by leveraging the shared knowledge between related tasks (103, 104). An example of this approach can be found in the work by Dai et al. (105), where a single model was trained to predict multiple reaction outcomes, such as yield and selectivity, in addition to retrosynthetic routes.

The application of ML and DL in retrosynthetic analysis has shown significant promise in overcoming the limitations of manual methods and traditional computational approaches, enabling the development of more accurate, efficient, and scalable tools for synthetic route prediction (106, 107).

References:

1. Gao, H., Struble, T. J., Coley, C. W., Wang, Y., Green, W. H., & Jensen, K. F. (2020). Using machine learning to predict organic reaction outcomes: Are we there yet? Chemical Science, 11(44), 12119-12130.
