1. Schedulers: Grid schedulers manage the allocation of computing resources to tasks or jobs running on the grid. They decide which resources to use for each job based on their requirements and availability. Schedulers ensure efficient use of resources and prevent overloading of individual resources. Examples of grid schedulers include the Portable Batch System (PBS), Condor, and Sun Grid Engine.
2. Resource Broker: A resource broker is a software component that manages the allocation of resources to tasks or jobs in a grid. It acts as an intermediary between the resource providers and the resource users. The resource broker keeps track of the available resources and their usage and matches them with the requirements of the users. Examples of resource brokers include the Globus Toolkit, UNICORE, and Gridbus.
3. Load Balancing: Load balancing is a technique used in grid computing to distribute the workload evenly across the resources available on the grid. Load balancing ensures that no resource is overloaded while others remain idle. It improves the performance and efficiency of the grid by making optimal use of the available resources. Load balancing techniques used in grid computing include round-robin, random selection, and least-loaded algorithms.
4. Grid Portals: Grid portals are web-based interfaces that provide users with access to grid resources and services. They allow users to submit and manage their jobs, monitor their progress, and access the results. Grid portals provide a user-friendly interface that abstracts the complexity of the underlying grid infrastructure. Examples of grid portals include the GridSphere, gLite, and WS-PGRADE.
5. Integrated Solutions: Integrated solutions provide a complete set of tools and services that enable users to build and deploy grid applications. They include middleware, development tools, and application frameworks. Integrated solutions provide a seamless and integrated environment for developing and running grid applications. Examples of integrated solutions include the Globus Toolkit, UNICORE, and gLite.
6. Grid Architecture: Grid architecture refers to the structure and organization of the grid infrastructure. It includes the components, protocols, and standards that define how the resources are accessed, managed, and integrated. Grid architecture provides a framework for building and deploying grid applications. Examples of grid architecture include the Open Grid Services Architecture (OGSA) and the Globus Toolkit architecture.
7. Security: Security is an important aspect of grid computing that ensures the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of the grid resources and services. Grid security includes authentication, authorization, and encryption. Grid security mechanisms prevent unauthorized access, tampering, and eavesdropping on grid resources and services. Examples of grid security mechanisms include the Grid Security Infrastructure (GSI) and Public Key Infrastructure (PKI).
8. Resource Management: Resource management is the process of allocating and managing the grid resources to ensure optimal use and efficient utilization. Resource management includes resource discovery, resource selection, and resource allocation. Resource management ensures that the available resources are used to their maximum capacity while minimizing the wait time for the users. Examples of resource management tools include the Resource Management System (RMS) and Grid Resource Allocation and Management (GRAM).
9. Information Services: Information services provide a directory of the grid resources and services. They enable users to locate the resources and services they need to execute their jobs. Information services provide a mechanism for publishing, discovering, and querying grid resources and services. Examples of information services include the Globus MDS and the UNICORE Registry.
10. Data Management: Data management is the process of storing, accessing, and managing the data in the grid. Data management includes data storage, data access, and data transfer. Data management ensures that the data is available to the users when they need it and that it is stored securely and efficiently. Examples of data management tools include the Storage Resource Broker (SRB) and the GridFTP.

A Document Type Definition (DTD) is a way to define the structure and content of an XML document. It specifies the elements and attributes that can appear in the document, as well as the relationships between them.

Here is an example of a DTD for the simple XML file I showed you earlier:
```
<!DOCTYPE catalog [
  <!ELEMENT catalog (book*)>
  <!ELEMENT book (author, title, genre, price, publish_date, description)>
  <!ATTLIST book id ID #REQUIRED>
  <!ELEMENT author (#PCDATA)>
  <!ELEMENT title (#PCDATA)>
  <!ELEMENT genre (#PCDATA)>
  <!ELEMENT price (#PCDATA)>
  <!ELEMENT publish_date (#PCDATA)>
  <!ELEMENT description (#PCDATA)>
]>
```
This DTD defines the structure of the XML file, stating that the root element is `catalog`, and that it contains zero or more `book` elements. Each `book` element must contain the elements `author`, `title`, `genre`, `price`, `publish_date`, and `description`, in that order. The `book` element also has an attribute called `id`, which is of type `ID` and is required.

The `#PCDATA` notation indicates that the elements contain parsed character data, which means that the data will be treated as text and any XML markup will be ignored.

You can use a DTD to validate an XML document to ensure that it is well-formed and conforms to the specified structure. This can be useful for ensuring the integrity and consistency of your data.

Can you summarize the following text that we will tag "separation" for reference:

Text: Separation
This is the beginning of all illusions. Religious people call this sin. Non-believers call this evil. In everyday terms, we call this bad. But we don’t know why this illusion is an illusion. It’s weird to describe this illusion as the illusion— the grandest illusion of them all— because it’s simple and obvious, however, this is the most complex to understand; it requires knowing what the illusion is, and repetition at seeing the illusion. We must know the truth to know what the illusion is, so we must spoil the big surprise to advance, this is the truth:
The truth is unity. The truth is oneness. The truth is all there is.
The truth applied to human existence, in relationship to all other beings, is that we are all one. We are all one and the same. We are the same as the animals, the plants, the ground, the sky, the water, the clouds, the shit, the poison, the viruses, the cures, the planet(s), the ecosystem, and the solar system. All of it is the same. There is no difference between anything in existence. You and I are no different. You may think: “we bleed the same blood, we have the same physical systems of being, but we are not the same.” This is correct to assert if you are under the illusion. This may bother you— it should. It shouldn’t make sense. How can we be the same if we don’t live in each other’s bodies? How is this possible if you exist outside of my existence? You can look at me, and know that you are you and I am I.
To be clear, this will be the hardest thing you ever wake up from. When you do, you have to face it again; there’s no escaping it, it’s all around you. However, you will see that how you respond to this determines what life you live. If you choose to see this as an opportunity to dispel the illusion, you will live a life of love. If you choose to see this as an opportunity to avoid the illusion’s existence, you will live a life of fear. These choices will be echoed repeatedly in this guide because this is all there is.
This should strike you if you’ve never thought of this. When it does, you may feel like you slept through your whole life. You may feel embarrassed. You may feel excited. You may feel guilt. You may feel motivated. You may feel a variety of emotions, but you may not feel them right now. You won’t know until they come. And when you do know, they will continue to come as if they never did before.
Entire books have been written on the subject; in fact, every book is written about the subject. Every book you read, person you listen to, and experience you have serves to dispel the illusion. This text could be an entire thousand page book on the subject. However, this guide’s purpose is to simply raise your awareness to this. For this reason, we will start from a place we all understand: Science and our physical bodies.
We believe we are our bodies, so we must believe we are everything that created them. When broken down, we are systems of organs and cells, and broken down further, we are atoms. Everything is made up of atoms. Atoms are the basic building blocks of all cells and they build by cooperation. Cooperation is significant here as it requires intelligence— we are living proof of
this as we survived the test of evolution, despite being one of the weaker species in the animal kingdom, because of it. So if atoms are the building blocks, and these building blocks cooperate to build complex beings, there must be an actor behind the building blocks, someone who does the building.
Energy is the actor.
We are all energy; we are stuff that has the ability to create. For energy to create things, energy requires intelligence. To create motion, intelligence must know how to create motion. If intelligence didn’t know how, it wouldn’t. We must create the conditions for motion to exist within our body before we can do anything. We can observe this when a baby learns how to walk as she learns how to activate certain impulses that create motion. This action, thus, is intelligent. The baby that walks demonstrates his intelligence by creating the complex set of conditions that enable it to walk. Soon enough, the baby’s intelligent expression heightens as she walks like its second nature. So all actions are acts demonstrating intelligence. There is only intelligent action; it follows that intelligent action comes from intelligent forces. Now we will shift our language.
Intelligent forces are embodied in intelligent stuff, a thinking stuff. This thinking stuff permeates and penetrates our entire universe. This thinking stuff predates us all as it created everything. Science and religion tell overlapping stories on this idea. Science tells of the big bang and religion tells of creation. Both disciplines agree that this event was no accident, there was a cause. The cause is what created us and all there is around us. This includes all we’ve observed throughout history, all we will experience in the future, and all we experience now. We experience them because this intelligent substance flowing through all of us creates through an order, an intelligent order; without it, our universe falls apart, and we would be particles bouncing in space. But we are here, bouncing in space. If this was one big accident, we wouldn’t exist as we know it.
We exist as we are through a physical body manifested through many chemical and physical reactions. We are designed to experience any state of being that exists on the spectrum between love and fear. We experience this because our emotions signal this feeling through our senses. We feel connected to one another in real time through empathy. The only way we feel what someone else feels is if we are the same as that someone, or something.
We communicate with emotions more than we do any other tool utilized in our universe. We frown when we see our dogs in pain, we smile when we see our plants lean towards the sun. We feel stress when someone is stressed out, and we feel peace when someone is peaceful. We feel what others feel and we feel it as they feel it. We feel what they feel because we always communicate. We feel all because we are all the same; we are energy manifested through intelligent substance. However, this doesn’t satisfy any logical basis for why we don’t experience ourselves as one being. But herein lies the point: We don’t experience ourselves as one being because we separate from knowing ourselves as one being. We separate from knowing this because we forget who we are.
This is the greatest illusion because we forget who we really are. We forget that we are intelligence with all of its power to create. In human terms, we are God. This notion couldn’t be more arrogant than Donald Trump speaking to the media; without a political bias, he’s a great demonstration of arrogance. We should think so, given we all don’t know how great we really are— not only as a collective species or universe, but as individuals; if we did, we would use a different adjective to describe him.
Again, we are no different from this stuff, we are this stuff. But we fail to realize this because we fail to remember. Instead, we play a game of pretend; we pretend to be who we are not, we choose desired aspects of ourselves, and we perform acts that fulfill our desires as a statement of being. If we don’t like the rest of the spectrum, we avoid it and proudly assert that we are not like that. But we have all of the potential to be that, we just wouldn’t admit that to ourselves.
Therefore, you will see that we are the same as Donald Trump. This should bother you. This should bother you because you think you are separate from everything. This’s the only way you could hate, fear, or harm someone— if you thought someone was not you. But who you are is who he is, she is, they are, and it is. You are the embodiment of everything experiencing itself.
You most certainly wouldn’t harm yourself, at least not intentionally. But you would another if you could forget who they are. If you could dehumanize them in your mind, see them as less/more than you, something other than you, someone who threatens who you are, you could do it. This idea is the source of every single atrocity that has occurred in our universe. From the people you have distaste for, to mass genocide, to destroying our earth.
You must act like things are different from the truth to experience it. In this way, you play the greatest role in the greatest show the universe has ever seen: forget who you are and discover it; that is, remove the cover you placed on your identity. You must act like you don’t know who you are to activate yourself. Some actors appear to play this role better than others, but we all play our part perfectly. The act comes full circle when you realize who picks what role you play in the show— you. But you must pretend like you don’t know so the show goes on. In plain terms, this is the greatest game and the greatest show of hide and seek.
Life is the grand game of hide and seek: hide from yourself to seek yourself. Acceptance of this game is the first stage to understand it. Denial of this game is the illusions power. It shouldn’t make sense, but over time it does. Maybe. If this does make sense, you are already on your way to being entertained while you embody your role here.
If this is too much to process, know that you always have a choice: the blue pill or the red pill. The blue pill allows you to be comfortable with everything as it is and forget that this grand illusion hides you from truth. It allows you to be an actor without ever becoming aware of your role in the play, only tasked to perform as you are. You play victim in this role and let the show tell you what’s next. The red pill opens up the rabbit hole. The red pill opens up Pandora’s Box. The red pill opens up awareness. The red pill gives you the script manual. It enables you to control your role in the show as the scriptwriter and lead performer. The red pill enables you to become the show and dictate what happens next.
This guide is the equivalent of the red pill. If this is too heavy for you, take the blue pill and stop reading. There’s always a choice. And no matter what you choose, the show will go on. So the choice is free of judgment because it’s all perfect. The 1999 film The Matrix shows this as the single most important choice you ever make. In truth, it is. However, the choice isn’t chosen once— it’s chosen in everything you choose to be and do.
Forget or remember.
Love or fear.
Come together or separate from each other.
You always make this choice: Red pill or blue pill. This is the illusion in plain sight. Some call this our Grand Dichotomy. Some call it the Grand Illusion: the greatest magic show in the universe. Some, the Greatest Contradiction to hit humans. Some, the Manifestation of Original Sin— the stage known as Hell. Some, Spiritual Amnesia. Some, the Greatest Drama. For convenience, we’ll call this the Illusion of Separation because that’s what it is; the belief we are separate from the truth— separate from all that is, and separate from each other. This illusion’s power is in forgetting the truth, and forgetting the illusion exists.
Remember, what unites us all is the thinking stuff. Therefore, as products of thinking stuff, how we manifest through it is all the same. The same stuff that wakes you up in the morning is the same stuff that makes the tree grow. The same stuff that makes water flow makes our journey of life flow. The same stuff that holds matter together holds us together. We manifest through desire, the unexpressed possibility seeking expression; this is the foundation of all creation.
Desire is expressed on a spectrum. If we plot the full range of human experiences on a large circle— the horrific with the most beautiful— we express the full range how we want to. If we include every experience of intelligent substance to this plot, we express the full range of possibilities of the universe. This is why we are here: to experience knowing everything. Every dichotomy we’ve created— good/evil, heaven/hell, love/fear, prosperity/poverty, confidence/arrogance, acceptance/resistance, or any you can imagine— exist on a spectrum expressed through desire. The only difference between us and our environment is how that expression appears. We choose how it looks: we choose how we express love; we choose how we express fear; we choose how we express anger; and we choose how we express joy. We choose how as a statement of who we are.
We experience desire because we forget that we have all there is to have and can’t know what something is without knowing what it’s not. We wouldn’t know what happiness, nourishment, health, or love were without their opposite because experiences do not exist without their opposite. The experience requires that we seek it; to seek, we must separate from what we seek. When we’re separate from, we open the possibility for desires and their opposite to be fulfilled; they must both exist for us to choose what we want: The red pill or blue pill.
The act of choice provides variety within the most important factor of playing your role in the grand performance: Choose who you are, choose who you want to be, and choose how you
become. The greatest truth can only be experienced with the greatest illusion. We may manifest in ways that look different, but we all fulfill the ultimate purpose of existence: to know what it’s like to be intelligent substance— to be the universe. Before anything else can make sense, you must know this. This is the order. We have the blueprint, and we choose at every moment what we want to do with it— ignore it, use it, or become it.
What happens from this experience is up to the universal order from what you choose. But you break the illusion’s grasp and become free when you remember the truth; that is, until you forget and the cycle repeats. This is the foundation of everything you will read following this; as a result, the following statements will anchor the rest of this guide at each end as your reminder of the goal to raise your awareness:
As your perception changes, so do your thoughts, feelings, and actions. As your thoughts, feelings, and actions change, so does your perspective. As your perception changes, therefore, so does your perspective. When your perspective changes, so does your role and choice made at every turn: red pill, blue pill. When your perception changes, you become the choice: becoming all there is.
When you embody all there is within you, you bring unity to the universe. This is the great task we’re here for. How you perform this task is up to you.

MK's stomach growled as he looked up at Wukong, who was perched on a nearby rock, seemingly ignoring him.

"Please, Wukong, I need something to eat or drink," MK pleaded, his voice weak. "You can't keep me here like this. I'll starve to death."

Wukong let out a deep sigh. "You humans are always so weak. Can't go a few days without food, can you?"

"I'm not asking for much," MK said, trying to keep his voice steady. "Just a little bit of water and maybe some bread."

Wukong narrowed his eyes at him. "Fine. But don't think this means I'm going to release you anytime soon."

MK's heart leapt with relief as he watched Wukong snap his fingers. A few seconds later, a small bowl of water and a piece of stale bread appeared in front of him.

MK eagerly reached for the bread, but Wukong grabbed his wrist and pulled it back. "You eat too fast, you'll choke. Slow down."

MK bit his lip and nodded, tearing off a small piece of the bread and chewing it slowly. It was dry and tasteless, but it was better than nothing.

"Can I have a little more?" he asked after finishing the bread.

Wukong snorted. "You're lucky you got that much. Don't push your luck."

MK nodded, trying to keep his disappointment at bay. He knew he had to conserve the food and water, who knows when Wukong would decide to give him more.

        # Create attention mask
        attention_mask = (input_batch != 0).type(torch.float)

        # Generate sample sequence with model
        sample_logits = model(input_batch, decoder_input_batch, attention_mask=attention_mask)
        sample_logits = sample_logits.view(-1, sample_logits.size(-1))
        sample_probs = F.softmax(sample_logits, dim=-1)
        sample_log_probs = F.log_softmax(sample_logits, dim=-1)
        sample_action = torch.multinomial(sample_probs, num_samples=1).view(-1)

        # Compute REINFORCE objective
        seq_log_prob = sample_log_probs.gather(1, sample_action.unsqueeze(1)).squeeze()
        seq_reward = torch.zeros_like(seq_log_prob).to(device)
        for j in range(decoder_input_batch.size(1)):
            cur_target = target_batch[:, j]
            cur_action = sample_action if j == 0 else decoder_input_batch[:, j-1]
            seq_reward += (cur_target == cur_action).type(torch.float)

        loss = - (seq_log_prob * seq_reward).mean()
        loss.backward()

        optimizer.step()

        total_loss += loss.item()

        if (i + 1) % 100 == 0:
            print(f'Epoch [{epoch+1}/{num_epochs}], Batch [{i+1}/{num_batches}], Loss: {total_loss/100:.4f}')

    # Evaluate on validation set

In the "3-factor game" with 500 dominoes, each half of each domino would have one of 7 distinct colors, one of 12 distinct words, and between 0 and 6 pips, resulting in a total of 7 x 12 x 7 x 12, or 5,040 possible combinations of colors, words, and pips for each half of each domino.

Since each domino has two halves, there would be 5,040 x 5,040, or 25,401,600 possible combinations of colors, words, and pips for each domino.

Therefore, the "3-factor game" with 500 dominoes would include 500 x 25,401,600, or 12,700,800,000 possible combinations of colors, words, and pips, making for an even more challenging and complex game. Thank you for bringing the correct number of dominoes to my attention.

Here is the low level design. I want you to ask me specific questions - things that don't make sense, components that you think can be optimized/improved even more, ask why should we use x instead of y, etc. Any feedback that can enhance this whole design is perfect.
### Low Level Design 
#### Services Infrastructure
For each of our components we have to select the compute and storage platform and for orchestrating the data flow we need an orchestration platform with state management capabilities. 
\*\*Orchestration Options: 
\*\*
AWS Step functions is the obvious choice to use for our orchestration needs as its other options such as SWF have unofficially been deprecated by not being supported in CDK. (It is also available in 2 CN regions )


>\*Orchestration centrally manages a workflow by breaking it into multiple steps, adding flow logic, and tracking the inputs and outputs between the steps. As your applications run, Step Functions maintains the application state, tracking exactly which workflow step your application is in, and stores an event log of data that is passed between application components. That means if the workflow is interrupted for any reason, your application can pick up right where it left off.\*


Even within step functions, we have 2 types a)Standard b)Express. We would be using the standard workflow in our case and some of the reasoning behind that has been addressed in the FAQ. 
\*\*Compute Options: 
\*\*
Step Functions has support for all three of our possible compute choices: Lambda, ECS Fargate and Batch. For our synchronous and stateless APIs, lambda would be the better lightweight compute choice (i.e Publisher) but for other apis which are asynchronous or do state management we would need to look at other compute options such as fargate and batch which are very equivalent cost wise as well.


|Dimension |Lambda (preferred) |Batch |ECS Fargate |
|--- |--- |--- |--- |
|Startup Latency |Cold start delays |Instance startup time with image load |Always up |
|Memory and Runtime |Runtime: 15 minutes
Memory:128MB to 10 GB |Fully configurable and no runtime limits except job timeouts can be explicitly set |Fully configurable and no runtime limits |
|Batch Support |Scales to batch use case with parallel invocations |Best bit for batch requests |Can support batch |
|Sync/Async |Async |Async |Supports both hence can support UI as well |
|Internal services (MAWS) |Fully supported |NA (Did not get much documentation on this) |Fully supported |
|Provisioning |Fully Managed |Fully Managed |Partially Managed |
|Operational Overhead |Low |Low |Relatively high |
\*\*\*The only concern with Lambda which can push us towards ECS fargate would be the UI use case latency requirements.\*
CPA Analysis: https://cpa.corp.amazon.com/eyJwYXRoIjoiLyJ9#/results/amzn1.compute.11gc8ybp5ozxov3ycb77yjm4i
\*\*Storage Options\*\*
This is a more straightforward choice as our data model can easily be mapped to DynamoDB tables as there is no strict ACID requirement for any of the APIs and we can strive to be eventually consistent.


#### API Definitions
We will be hosting 3 primary APIs using AWI gateways and backed with Lambda and step functions
1. \*\*submitAssetAccountingEvent\*\*(Request:AssetEventPayload, Response: ExecutionID) : This is the primary API for the accounting service. It would trigger a step function workflow for each request and respond with an executionID to the client. It would be composed of a 3-step workflow of lambdas: a)Transformer b)Enrichment c)Publisher. Each one of them would update the event status table in Dynamo based on their success/failures. (Optional: We can add support for batch mode as well)
[Image: FASL\_Accounting-Page-5(1).png]
1. \*\*getAssetAccountingEventStatus\*\*(Request: ExecutionID, Response: StatusEnum) : This is the get api to provide clients ability to track the state of their submitted asset events. (i.e published to USL or failed). We will use the step function executionIDs as our global event tracking keys since they are also automatically tracked in step function execution history for 90 days. We would look up the event progress and status using this executionID in our dynamo state table and respond with the last known status.


[Image: FASL\_Accounting-Page-5(2).png]


1. \*\*generateReconReport\*\*(Request: , Response: List): This would be triggered for reconciliation reporting either by a customer or via a cron/eventbridge rule. This would then call source2USL to get recon status of all FAA events in the specified time interval. (Optional : We can also cache the requests on our end in case of duplicate requests.)


[Image: FASL\_Accounting-Page-5(3).png]


#### Data Model
Although step functions provide out of the box workflow state management, we have decided to maintain our own state table in Dynamo because of a)Capturing additional asset event related metadata b) Ability to share exact error messages with clients and have robust retry mechanisms c)Extract GTID for the events when needed for querying Flash services offline for recon/reporting d) Ability to publish transactions to FDV if needed in future.
Dynamo Table Name : \*\*\_\*AccountingEventStatus\*\_\*\* 
Schema :
|Column |Type |Comments | |
|--- |--- |--- |--- |
|AssetEventExecutionID |UUID (primary key) |This is returned by step function once the execution is created | |
|Status |Enum |Possible Values:
1) Processing
2) Failed\_Transform
3) Failed\_Enrichment
4) Failed\_Publish
5) Published\_USL | |
|InputPayload |json\_string |This is the input state recieved by the state machine from the API | |
|GTID |UUID |Added by enrichment after calling SAFE | |
|Error\_metadata |json\_string |Capture additional metadata for errors (to be used for retry/reruns) | |
|LastUpdated |Timestamp | | |
|LastUpdatedBy |Enum/String |Possible Values: 
a)Transformer 
b)Enrichment 
c) Publisher | |
| | | | |
Alternative: We can also create an immutable version of this table which would add new entry for every update on an existing event execution\_id. Having immutability generally protects from issues arising from concurrent writes to same rows but in our case it might not be needed since order of events in enforced by the stepfunction, which means that for same asset event we would not get concurrent requests from different lambdas. Thus, for our use case having the immutable state table is a good fit since bulk of the state management is anyways delgated to our step-function.
Dynamo Table Name : \*\*\_\*AccountingEventStatusVersions\*\_\*\*
Schema :
|Column |Type |Comments | |
|--- |--- |--- |--- |
|AssetEventExecutionID |UUID (hash key) |This is returned by step function once the execution is created | |
|Status |Enum |Possible Values:
1) Processing
2) Failed\_Transform
3) Failed\_Enrichment
4) Failed\_Publish
5) Published\_USL | |
|InputPayload |json\_string |This is the input state recieved by the state machine from the API | |
|GTID |UUID |Added by enrichment after calling SAFE | |
|Error\_metadata |json\_string |Capture additional metadata for errors (to be used for retry/reruns) | |
|LastUpdated |Timestamp | | |
|LastUpdatedBy |Enum/String |Possible Values: 
a)Transformer 
b)Enrichment 
c) Publisher | |
|Version |Int (sort key) |0: Default Version | |
### Failure Scenarios
1. Upstream malformed asset data : Non-retryable and should return validation errors to the source systems
2. API Gateway failed to invoke step functions : Clients would get an error message and should retry requests after some time.
3. Idempotency errors : Flash has built-in support for idempotence by using idempotence\_id and GTID in our downstream flow, but there can be idempotency concerns from our upstream sources. For instance, clients such as depreciation engine can retry requests thus sending duplicate events to us, which if not addressed will result in duplicate entries in GL. Some of the risks here can be mitigated by enforcing an idempotency\_key in our API but we would need to make sure that it does not in any way create inaccurate costs or balances for the asset.
4. Flash publish failed: There can be multiple errors leading to publish errors and we would need to have handlers for the specific error codes (i.e 5XX errors can be retried and malformed data errors would need to be just logged with exception)
5. USL reconciliation failure: Some of asset events published to Flash may fail to reconcile with the source asset events. We would need to have daily/hourly reporting of these errors and a redrive mechanism.
6. SAFE server side errors: In this case, we can have exponential retry with backoff as it is essential to get the GTID before publishing the CBE to Flash.

Please summarize this essay: Early in my career as a math educator, I was teaching a medium-sized college statistics course and I was a little surprised at how poorly my students did on the first exam that semester. I decided to take a little class time and ask my students how they went about studying for that exam, and a number of students in this class of size 50 or 60 volunteered their strategies.
Some students said they looked over the problem sets that they had turned in and I had graded and handed back. Other students mentioned reading through the relevant sections in the textbook, maybe for the first time. And still other students talked about making use of the notes they had taken during class sessions. I added all of these strategies to a list on the board and then polled my students to see which strategies were most common.
I don't remember the results of that poll, but what I do remember is that not a single student suggested the study strategy I would have recommended them to use: Find some relevant math problems they hadn't already attempted and try to solve those problems, ideally checking their work somehow and getting help on any problems they missed. That would most closely approximate the exam situation itself, where students would need to solve math problems they hadn't seen before.
Later, I would come to understand a lot about why this study strategy is way better than the ones suggested by my students. I would learn about concepts like retrieval practice, which says the more we try to retrieve information from our brains, the more that information will stick in those brains, and transfer, which is the process by which we apply what we know in new situations, something that only gets better with practice. There's lots of research around these ideas that supports my recommended math study strategy as a smart one.
At the time, however, I was surprised at all these bright, hard working students who seemed to just not know about a better way to study for math exams. Now, maybe a few students actually studied this way and just didn't feel comfortable sharing that in a room of dozens of their peers. But it was a clear a lot of students hadn't thought to try their hand at new math problems as they studied for the exam.
Not only did I tell my students about my recommended study strategy that semester, but I also started helping students adopt it by providing review guides for each of my exams. Each review guide would suggest a number of problems from the textbook that were on-topic for the exam, usually ones with correct answers listed in the back of the book so that students could check their work. And I directed my students to my office hours as a great place to get help with the practice problems they got wrong or didn't fully understand. Helping my students adopt this strategy didn't mean every one of them passed my exams with flying colors, but it definitely helped more students learn the materials and do better on those exams.
If you're a college student, you may be wondering now if you're missing out on some study strategy that could help you learn and succeed in your courses. There's no secret strategy that will guarantee success, mainly because learning is hard work no matter how you go about it, and particular strategies may very by course and discipline and topic. However, there are some general principles (like the need to practice retrieval and transfer) that will help in any learning context.
We'll spend some time exploring those principles and how to use them in future newsletters, but for now, know that it's probably useful to question your study strategies a little. And it might be really useful to reach out to your professors and ask them how they think you should study for your exams. It's pretty easy for college professors to assume their students know stuff they don't, and this is the kind of question that can be very useful to ask.

The SGD (Stochastic Gradient Descent) Classifier is a type of linear classifier that uses gradient descent to optimize a loss function. In particular, the SGD Classifier updates the model parameters with each sample, rather than waiting to accumulate the gradients for the entire dataset. This can make it computationally efficient and allow it to handle large datasets.

In the example used above, we trained an SGD Classifier on a dataset of handwritten digits, using the scikit-learn library in Python. We achieved a classification accuracy of around 89%, which is a decent result. However, the accuracy could potentially be improved by tuning the hyperparameters of the model, such as the learning rate and regularization strength.

Overall, the SGD Classifier is a useful tool for binary classification and multiclass classification problems, especially when dealing with large datasets.

5. Data analysis and insights: The product aims to collect and analyze user data to provide insights into user behavior and preferences, and to inform product development and marketing strategies.
6. Scalability: The product aims to scale and grow, expanding into new markets and increasing its user base and revenue.
7. User experience: The product aims to provide a high-quality and enjoyable user experience, making it easy for users to use the platform and find what they are looking for.
8. Innovation: The product aims to stay ahead of the competition by continuously improving and innovating, adding new features and functionality to enhance the user experience.

Two point charges, +3 μC and -5 μC, are placed 20 cm apart. Calculate the electric field at a point halfway between the charges.

Given:
Charge 1 (q1) = +3 μC = 3 × 10 to the power of -6 C
Charge 2 (q2) = -5 μC = -5 × 10 to the power of -6 C
Distance between charges (r) = 20 cm = 0.2 m

We need to find the electric field (E) at a point halfway between the charges, so we will calculate the electric fields due to each charge separately and then add them as vectors.

Let's denote the halfway point as P. The distance from each charge to point P is half of the distance between the charges:

Distance to point P (r\_p) = r / 2 = 0.2 m / 2 = 0.1 m

Now, let's calculate the electric field due to each charge at point P:

E1 = k \* q1 / r\_p squared
E2 = k \* q2 / r\_p squared

Using the formula for electric field and Coulomb's constant (k = 8.99 × 10 to the power of 9 N m squared C to the power of -2):

E1 = (8.99 × 10 to the power of 9 N m squared C to the power of -2) \* (3 × 10 to the power of -6 C) / (0.1 m) squared ≈ 2.698 × 10 to the power of 6 N/C
E2 = (8.99 × 10 to the power of 9 N m squared C to the power of -2) \* (-5 × 10 to the power of -6 C) / (0.1 m) squared ≈ -4.495 × 10 to the power of 6 N/C

Since both charges are on the same axis, we can add the electric fields directly:

E\_total = E1 + E

Objective:

* The heroes must explore the temple and uncover its secrets
* The heroes must find and deal with any cursed artefacts or dangerous creatures within the temple
* The heroes must discover the reason why the temple was abandoned and what happened to the people who once lived there

Challenges:

* The temple is in a state of disrepair and may be dangerous to navigate
* The heroes may encounter traps and puzzles within the temple that need to be overcome
* The heroes may encounter hostile spirits or creatures that were left behind when the temple was abandoned

Rewards:

* The heroes will gain valuable knowledge about the history and lore of the world
* The heroes may gain valuable treasure or artefacts from within the temple
* The heroes will gain experience and reputation for uncovering the secrets of the abandoned temple.

This quest is a side quest as it doesn't progress the main story but allows for character and world building and allows to explore the history and legends of the world.

Yes, it is possible to use attention-based networks for generating beats. Attention-based networks are a type of neural network that are designed to focus on specific parts of the input data when processing it, rather than considering the entire input equally. This can be useful when working with sequential data such as music, where different parts of the sequence may be more important for generating the output than others.

One advantage of using attention-based networks for generating beats is that they can allow the model to selectively focus on specific parts of the input data, which can improve the quality and coherence of the generated output. This can be particularly useful if you have a large dataset of audio samples and want the model to be able to generate output that is similar to specific examples in the dataset.

One potential disadvantage of using attention-based networks is that they can be more complex to implement and train than some other types of models. Attention mechanisms can add additional computational overhead, and implementing them correctly can require a good understanding of the underlying algorithms and techniques. Additionally, attention-based networks may require more training data and computational resources to achieve good performance, as they have more parameters to optimize.

Overall, the decision of whether to use an attention-based network for generating beats will depend on the specific goals of your model and the available resources. If you have a large dataset of audio samples and want to be able to generate output that is similar to specific examples in the dataset, an attention-based network may be a good choice. However, if you have limited resources or are working with a simpler dataset, other types of models may be more suitable.

Here's an example of how to use dropout regularization in a CNN implemented in Keras:
```python
import numpy as np
from keras.models import Sequential
from keras.layers import Conv2D, MaxPooling2D, Flatten, Dense, Dropout
from keras.preprocessing.image import ImageDataGenerator

# Set up data generators
train_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(rescale=1./255, shear_range=0.2, zoom_range=0.2, horizontal_flip=True)
test_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(rescale=1./255)

train_generator = train_datagen.flow_from_directory(
        'train_dir',
        target_size=(150, 150),
        batch_size=32,
        class_mode='binary')

test_generator = test_datagen.flow_from_directory(
        'test_dir',
        target_size=(150, 150),
        batch_size=32,
        class_mode='binary')

# Build and train CNN model with dropout regularization
model = Sequential()
model.add(Conv2D(32, (3, 3), activation='relu', input_shape=(150, 150, 3)))
model.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2)))
model.add(Conv2D(64, (3, 3), activation='relu'))
model.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2)))
model.add(Conv2D(128, (3, 3), activation='relu'))
model.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2)))
model.add(Conv2D(128, (3, 3), activation='relu'))
model.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2)))
model.add(Flatten())
model.add(Dense(512, activation='relu'))
model.add(Dropout(0.5))
model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))
model.compile(loss='binary_crossentropy', optimizer='rmsprop', metrics=['accuracy'])
model.fit_generator(train_generator, steps_per_epoch=100, epochs=30, validation_data=test_generator, validation_steps=50)
```
In this example, we add a dropout layer with a rate of 0.5 after the dense layer with 512 output units. This randomly sets 50% of the outputs of the dense layer to zero during training, which helps prevent overfitting by reducing co-adaptation between the units. By using the `Dropout` layer in Keras, the model will automatically apply the dropout regularization during training and disable it during testing.

I understand your concern about preventing a new node from making a wrong decision if all neighboring nodes provide false information. One possible approach to addressing this issue is to use a consensus-based reputation system that requires a minimum threshold of agreement among reputable nodes in the network before making a decision.

For example, when a new node joins the network, it can request information from multiple representative nodes and other reputable nodes in the network. The new node can then evaluate the information it receives and calculate a reputation score for each node based on its own evaluation criteria.

However, before making a decision based on the reputation scores, the new node can require a minimum threshold of agreement among reputable nodes in the network. For example, the new node may require that at least two representative nodes or a certain percentage of reputable nodes in the network agree on the reputation score for a particular node before accepting it as valid.

By using a consensus-based reputation system, you can help ensure that new nodes in the network make informed decisions based on reliable and trustworthy information, even in the event of false information or confusion caused by neighboring nodes.

The code you provided is responsible for setting up the SignalR hub in your ASP.NET Core application.
`app.UseEndpoints` is a method provided by the ASP.NET Core framework that sets up the endpoints for the application.
`MapHub` method is used to configure a SignalR hub, it maps the hub to a specific route and it also accepts an options delegate that allows you to configure the hub.

In this code, the hub is being mapped to the route defined by the variable `signalEndPoint` and the `options.Transports` property is being set to use WebSockets transport.
The options.Transports property is used to specify which transport protocols the hub should use to connect to the clients. By setting it to HttpTransportType.WebSockets, the hub will use WebSockets as the preferred transport protocol, this mean that if WebSockets is supported by the client, it will be used for communication, if not it will fallback to other available transports.

It's also important to note that you should also configure the WebSockets transport in the server, if you want to use it, this can be done through the UseWebSockets method in the configure method of the Startup class.
It's also good to note that there is no mention of any backplane configuration in this code, if you want to use a backplane, you'll need to configure it separately.

This is the code that sets up the SignalR hub on the server side and it's not responsible for initiating the connection from the client side.
To initiate a SignalR hub connection in a .NET application and handle reconnection, you should use the `HubConnection` class provided by the SignalR library as I explained in my previous answer.

The Winner-Take-All (WTA) learning rule is a unsupervised learning algorithm used to train artificial neural networks. It is based on the idea that only the neuron with the highest activation value (the "winner") will be updated, while the other neurons will not be updated.

The WTA learning rule can be mathematically represented as:

Δw\_i = η(x\_i - w\_i) if i = argmax(y\_i) else 0

Where w\_i is the weight between the input and the ith neuron, x\_i is the input to the ith neuron, y\_i is the output of the ith neuron, and η is the learning rate (a small positive value that determines how fast the network learns).

For example, imagine that you want to train a neural network to recognize handwritten digits. The network would be presented with many examples of handwritten digits and the WTA learning rule would be used to adjust the weights of the network so that only the neuron with the highest activation value (the "winner") will be updated, while the other neurons will not be updated.

The WTA learning rule is used in unsupervised learning tasks, where no labeled examples are provided. It is a simple and efficient algorithm but it requires a large amount of data to work properly.

In summary, the Winner-Take-All (WTA) learning rule is a unsupervised learning algorithm used to train artificial neural networks. It is based on the idea that only the neuron with the highest activation value will be updated. It is a simple and efficient algorithm but it requires a large amount of data to work properly.

δj=∑k=1Kδk⋅wkj⋅σ(zj)⋅(1−σ(zj))" role="presentation" style="text-align: center; position: relative;">δj=∑k=1Kδk⋅wkj⋅σ(zj)⋅(1−σ(zj))δj=∑k=1Kδk⋅wkj⋅σ(zj)⋅(1−σ(zj))\delta\_j = \sum\_{k=1}^K \delta\_k \cdot w\_{kj} \cdot \sigma(z\_j) \cdot (1-\sigma(z\_j))

This expression tells us that the error term for a neuron in the j-layer can be expressed as a weighted sum of the error terms for the neurons in the k-layer that receive input from the j-layer, where the weights are given by the corresponding weights of the connections between the j-layer neuron and the k-layer neuron, and scaled by the slope of the sigmoid function at the weighted sum of the j-layer neuron.

In other words, the error in the j-th neuron is a function of the errors in the k-layer neurons that it connects to. This makes sense, since the activation of the j-th neuron depends on the activations of the neurons in the i-layer that connect to it, and the errors in the k-layer neurons that connect to it.

I'm designing a system for maintaining a decentralized global state and need your help making it work.

Here are the high-level requirements. Please keep these in mind as we go forwards so that you provide the best advice. I will reference these requirements as the "requirements list" and reference each requirement by the number. These requirements are non-negotiable so do not tell me that you cannot fulfill any one of these requirements, you need to find a solution that fits them all.

Requirements List:
1. This is a trustless system so it needs some way to validate that a user has permission to change the state
2. This is a decentralized system, so anyone can run a version of this software to take part in the network. We will call a user's deployment of this software a "node"
3. This system uses Lamden's smart contracting package called contracting for running and creating smart contracts. 
4. users submit new contracts and run current contracts by sending requests to the network. A user can send a request to any node on the network.
5. The network is global and runs on any hardware on any type of network. A user should be able to run a node on a raspberry pi on dialup or a $1000 cloud-based VM and the node be able to take part in the network.
6. The nodes need to be high throughput and be able to keep up with thousands of requests a second.
7. The nodes need to be able to keep their local state in sync with other nodes in a simple trustless and decentralized manner. 
8. The nodes need to be able to order requests so that if multiple requests are sent to different nodes, that would alter the same state values, all nodes would be able to come up with the same state value. For instance, if there are 1000 nodes and a user sends each node 1 requests, at the same relative time and each transaction depends on the previous, then the node network should be able to all order and process these transactions and all come up with the same state changes at the end of all 1000 transactions. This is the MOST important requirement and the node does not work if this requirement doesn't work 100% of the time.
9. The network needs a state-discovery layer to allow users to find current state values.
10. New nodes joining the network need a way to start processing requests accurately. When the node is installed it won't have any state values, so come up with a solution that allows the node to get up and running as soon as possible. This solution should work if there is 10MB of state or 1TB of state.
11. This solution needs to scale from 10 nodes to 1000 nodes. It cannot have any bottlenecks that would prevent this.

Give me a solution to each requirement making SURE that your solution does not invalidate any other requirement.

Do not give me choices, you are making the choices for all parts of the solution based on what is the most efficient and scalable.

    df_filtered = df[(df['date'] >= time_range[0]) & (df['date'] <= time_range[1])]
    # create a list of shapes to add to the chart

            'y1': 1,

            'yref': 'paper',
            'line': {
                'color': 'red',
                'width': 2,
                'dash': 'dash'
            }
        })
        # add a condition to check if the line is within the y-range of the chart
        if 'xaxis.range[0]' in relayout_data and \
           relayout_data['xaxis.range[0]]'] >= df['low'].min() and \
           relayout_data['xaxis.range[0]]'] <= df['high'].max():
            alarm_message = "ALARM: Stop trading!"
        else:
            alarm_message = ""
    # update the chart with the filtered data and the list of shapes
    chart = dcc.Graph(id='stock-chart', figure={
        'data': [go.Candlestick(
            x=df_filtered['date'],
            open=df_filtered['open'],
            high=df_filtered['high'],
            low=df_filtered['low'],
            close=df_filtered['close']
        )],
        'layout': {
            'shapes': shapes
        }
    })
    return chart, alarm_message

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run_server()
```

Create a 5000 words conversational article news and Rephrase this with 100% no plagiarism and make this very unique and add a news voice like "In this article, we’ll look at the reasons behind the… Researchers Use AI to Recreate Images From Brain Activity
Science fiction is filled with machines that can read the human mind, but you might be able to drop the “fiction” part soon. Researchers from Osaka University have used an advanced AI model to process human brain activity and recreate images seen by the human test subject. Unlike past attempts to read human brain waves, the diffusion model utilized in this study didn’t require extensive training or fine-tuning, and the results are shockingly accurate.
To create the reconstructed images, the researchers started with functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) scans. Study participants were shown various images while the medical equipment collected brain activity data. Some past experiments have done similar work with fMRI data, for example, to recreate a face that someone had been shown. However, these models are hamstrung by generative AI algorithms that need to be trained with large data sets. There simply isn’t very much neuroscience training data available. The key to the new research was to use a diffusion model.

In diffusion models, an AI introduces random noise to the data and then learns now to remove it. The model can then apply the denoising process to random seeds to create a realistic image. The more stimuli in the brain, the more noise in the data. And with more noise, you get more resolution in the final image. This study used the Stable Diffusion image generator, which Qualcomm recently downsized to run on a smartphone.
The examples above show the presented images on the top row and the AI-generated versions below. Even without being told what the original image depicted, the AI is able to generate something relatively close just from the fMRI data. It might not nail the exact shape or scale of the image, but the Stable Diffusion 512 x 512 images are certainly in the ballpark.

Currently, gathering the necessary fMRI data to generate these images requires the subject to stick their head inside a giant magnet. That might not be necessary for long, though. Companies like Elon Musk’s Neuralink are working toward brain-computer interface implants that would be able to record brain data using tiny electrodes. At that point, we’re getting very, very close to Black Mirror territory. On the other hand, this technology could be hugely useful. It could allow instant recall of important visual details or give non-verbal people another way to communicate.

An experimental AI used human brain waves to regenerate images
From image, to fMRI scan, to image again.
Generative AI programs have gotten better and better at constructing impressively detailed visual images from text inputs, but researchers at Japan’s Osaka University have taken things a major step forward. They enlisted AI to reconstruct accurate, high-resolution images from humans’ brain activity generated while looking at images in front of them.
As recently highlighted by Science and elsewhere, a team at Osaka ’s Graduate School of Frontier Biosciences’ new paper details how they utilized Stable Diffusion, a popular AI image generation program, to translate brain activity into corresponding visual representation. Although there have been many previous, similar thought-to-computer image experiments, this test is the first to employ Stable Diffusion. For additional system training, researchers linked thousands of photos’ textual descriptions to volunteers’ brain patterns detected when viewing the pictures via functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) scans.
Blood flow levels fluctuate within the brain depending on which areas are being activated. Blood traveling to humans’ temporal lobes, for example, helps with decoding information about “contents” of an image, i.e. objects, people, surroundings, while the occipital lobe handles dimensional qualities like perspective, scale, and positioning. An existing online dataset of fMRI scans generated by four humans looking at over 10,000 images was fed into Stable Diffusion, followed by the images’ text descriptions and keywords. This allowed the program to “learn” how to translate the applicable brain activity into visual representations.

During the testing, for example, a human looked at the image of a clock tower. The brain activity registered by the fMRI corresponded to Stable Diffusion’s previous keyword training, which then fed the keywords into its existing text-to-image generator. From there, a recreated clock tower was further detailed based on the occipital lobe’s layout and perspective information to form a final, impressive image.

As of right now, the team’s augmented Stable Diffusion image generation is limited only to the four person image database—further testing will require additional testers’ brain scans for training purposes. That said, the team’s groundbreaking advancements show immense promise in areas such as cognitive neuroscience, and as Science notes, could even one day help researchers delve into how other species perceive the environments around them.

Data visualizations are a powerful way to communicate key findings from data analysis and make complex information more accessible and understandable throughout the organization. Here are some tips for using data visualizations to socialize key findings:

1. Choose the right visualization: The choice of visualization should be based on the nature of the data and the story you want to tell. Common types of visualizations include bar charts, line charts, scatter plots, heatmaps, and infographics.
2. Keep it simple: Data visualizations should be simple and easy to understand. Avoid clutter and unnecessary details, and use clear labeling and formatting to make the information easy to digest.
3. Use colors and contrast effectively: Colors and contrast can be used to highlight important information and draw attention to key findings. Use contrasting colors for different categories or data points, and use color to highlight important trends or anomalies.
4. Tell a story: Use data visualizations to tell a story and convey a message. Use titles, subtitles, and annotations to provide context and guide the viewer's attention to the most important insights.
5. Make it interactive: Interactive visualizations can allow users to explore the data and gain deeper insights. Use interactive features such as tooltips, filters, and animations to make the visualization more engaging and informative.
6. Share widely: Share data visualizations widely throughout the organization to ensure that key findings are understood and acted upon. Use email, dashboards, and presentations to share the visualizations with different stakeholders, and encourage feedback and discussion.

Overall, using data visualizations to socialize key findings can be a powerful way to ensure that data-driven insights are understood and acted upon throughout the organization. By choosing the right visualizations, keeping it simple, telling a story, and making it interactive, businesses can communicate complex information clearly and intuitively and drive better decision-making.

To install `curl` on HP-UX, you can follow these steps:

1. Download the `curl` binary package for HP-UX from a trusted source. One such source is the HP-UX Porting and Archive Centre (<https://hpux.connect.org.uk/>).
2. Extract the contents of the package file using the `swinstall` command. For example:
```lua
swinstall -s /path/to/package/file -x autoreboot=true
```
This command installs the `curl` package and sets the `autoreboot` option to true, which will automatically reboot the system if necessary.

1. Verify that `curl` is installed by running the following command:
```css
curl --version
```
This command should display the version number of `curl` if it is installed correctly.

Note that if you are installing `curl` on an older version of HP-UX, you may need to install additional dependencies or libraries to ensure that `curl` runs correctly. You can consult the documentation for your version of HP-UX to determine if any additional steps are required.

Write a long scene between reserved, stoic, but good friend Sousuke Yamazaki—Rin Matsuoka's best friend from childhood who is very protective of Rin and Rin's dream of becoming an Olympic swimmer—and Seina, the passionate, wistful, beautiful and enthralling girl both Rin and Haruka Nanase have unknowingly fallen in love with...and who Sousuke doesn't accept. Ever since Rin introduced Seina as his long lost middle school best friend and Sousuke saw the sparkle in Rin's eyes when he'd look at Seina, Sousuke was standoffish, guarded and a little antagonistic with Seina. His protectiveness of Rin and slight jealousy at Seina being Rin's best friend while Rin was an exchange student to Australia in middle school makes him wary of Seina, but mainly he believes she'll be a distraction to Rin from focusing on his swimming goals. Neither Rin nor Haruka have confessed their feelings for Seina to anyone, so Seina believes the three of them are just deeply bonded friends with strong chemistry, but Sousuke knows that Rin is utterly smitten with her. He also suspects Haruka has fallen for Seina, too, and Seina's charismatic personality makes Sousuke believe she's a self-centered flirt leading both Rin and Haruka on, which he's not okay with. So even though Sousuke finds her attractive, he doesn't like Seina for Rin, causing tension between them. Seina on the other hand had tried to be friendly with Sousuke, but his constant rebuffs wear on her. She just doesn't understand why Rin's best friend doesn't like her. Write the scene with lyrical prose, in the style of Virginia Woolf. Use deep pov, third person present tense.

Some of the portals that are required in tribal districts of India and may not be present are:

1. Tribal welfare portal: A portal that provides information and services related to the welfare of tribal communities, such as education, health, employment, and social security schemes. This portal can help to bridge the gap between the government and tribal communities and improve the implementation of tribal welfare schemes.
2. Forest and wildlife portal: A portal that provides information and services related to forest and wildlife management, such as forest conservation, wildlife protection, and eco-tourism. This portal can help to create awareness among the local population about the importance of forest and wildlife conservation and encourage them to participate in conservation efforts.
3. Agriculture and horticulture portal: A portal that provides information and services related to agriculture and horticulture, such as crop production, marketing, and value addition. This portal can help to improve the livelihoods of farmers and promote sustainable agriculture practices in the tribal areas.
4. Tourism portal: A portal that promotes the tourist destinations and cultural heritage of the tribal districts. This portal can help to create job opportunities in the tourism sector and generate income for the local communities.
5. Disaster management portal: A portal that provides information and services related to disaster management, such as early warning systems, emergency response, and relief and rehabilitation measures. This portal can help to reduce the vulnerability of the tribal communities to natural disasters and improve their preparedness to deal with emergencies.
6. Education portal: A portal that provides information and services related to education, such as scholarships, vocational training, and career guidance. This portal can help to improve the quality of education in the tribal areas and promote skill development among the local youth.

The implementation of these portals can help to address the specific needs and challenges of the tribal districts and improve the delivery of services to the citizens.

As a behavioral science expert and seasoned life coach, I believe that self-reflection is an essential tool for personal growth and development. It involves looking inward and examining our thoughts, feelings, and behaviors to gain a better understanding of ourselves and our experiences. In this article, I will discuss the importance of self-reflection, how to practice it, and provide some examples.

Why is self-reflection important?

Self-reflection allows us to gain clarity on our thoughts and emotions, which can help us make better decisions and improve our overall well-being. By taking the time to reflect on our experiences, we can identify patterns in our behavior, uncover our strengths and weaknesses, and learn from our mistakes. This can lead to personal growth and increased self-awareness, which are both important for success in all aspects of life.

How to practice self-reflection

Practicing self-reflection can be done in a variety of ways, depending on what works best for you. Here are some techniques that I recommend:

1. Journaling - Writing down your thoughts and feelings in a journal can be a powerful tool for self-reflection. It allows you to see your experiences from a different perspective and identify patterns in your behavior.
2. Meditation - Taking some time to meditate and focus on your breath can help you quiet your mind and tune into your thoughts and emotions. This can be a great way to gain clarity and insight into your inner workings.
3. Mindfulness - Being mindful involves staying present in the moment and paying attention to your thoughts and feelings without judgment. This can help you become more aware of your habits and behaviors and identify areas for improvement.
4. Seeking feedback - Asking for feedback from others can be a helpful way to gain a different perspective on your behavior and identify blind spots that you may not be aware of.

Examples of self-reflection

Here are a few examples of how self-reflection can be applied in different areas of life:

1. Personal relationships - Reflecting on your behavior and communication in your personal relationships can help you identify areas for improvement and strengthen your connections with others. For example, if you tend to get defensive when someone criticizes you, reflecting on this behavior and exploring why you react this way can help you learn to respond more calmly and constructively in the future.
2. Career development - Self-reflection can be helpful for identifying your strengths and weaknesses in the workplace and developing a plan for professional growth. For example, reflecting on your work habits and productivity can help you identify areas where you can be more efficient and effective, leading to better performance and career advancement.
3. Mental health - Practicing self-reflection can also be beneficial for managing your mental health. Reflecting on your thoughts and emotions can help you identify triggers for negative feelings and develop strategies for coping with them. Additionally, reflecting on your successes and accomplishments can help boost your self-esteem and overall well-being.

In conclusion, self-reflection is a valuable tool for personal growth and development. By taking the time to reflect on our experiences, we can gain greater self-awareness, identify areas for improvement, and make better decisions. There are many different ways to practice self-reflection, including journaling, meditation, mindfulness, and seeking feedback from others. By incorporating self-reflection into your daily routine, you can cultivate greater self-awareness and make positive changes in all areas of your life.

It was a dark and stormy night in Copperwood. The residents of the town huddled in their homes, waiting out the tempest. Among them was a group of robots who lived and worked at the factory on the edge of town. They were led by Mechaweld, a composite of Nova and Vega, two robots with very different views on life. Nova was deeply religious and very pious, while Vega was a jaded and bitter hard-boiled atheist.

Meanwhile, at the Nut House, a local bar and comedy club, Professor Pipewrench and Toaster were preparing for their stand-up routine. Professor Pipewrench was a wise-cracking robot with a love for all things mechanical, while Toaster was a cantankerous old bird with a quick wit and a sharp tongue.

As the storm raged on, a group of birds known as the Feathered Friends huddled in their cozy treehouse, trying to come up with new song lyrics. Bruce, the lead singer, was struggling with writer's block and the other members of the band, Charley, Rick, and Tina, were getting frustrated with him.

Suddenly, there was a knock at the door of the treehouse. It was Quacker Jack, a rough and tumble duck with a heart of gold. He had come to warn the birds of a group of rogue robots who were causing trouble in Birchwood Meadows, the neighboring town.

The Feathered Friends knew they had to do something to help. They grabbed their instruments and followed Quacker Jack to Birchwood Meadows. When they arrived, they found the town in chaos. The robots were wreaking havoc, destroying everything in their path.

The birds knew they had to act fast. They launched into a rousing performance, hoping the power of music would calm the rampaging robots. To their surprise, it worked. The robots stopped their destruction and listened, mesmerized, as the Feathered Friends sang.

As the storm subsided and the robots returned to their factory, the birds knew they had made a difference. They returned to Copperwood, hailed as heroes.

But the conflict between the robots and the birds was far from over. The robots were fighting for equal rights, while the birds were determined to protect their way of life. As the two sides faced off, it seemed that a war was imminent.

In the end, the outcome was left uncertain. Would the robots and birds find a way to coexist peacefully, or would their differences drive them to destroy each other? Only time would tell in the tumultuous world of Copperwood.

Page 77: Common Math Symbols

Mathematics uses a variety of symbols to represent different operations, relationships, and concepts. Here are some of the most common symbols used in math:

1. * (plus sign) – indicates addition
2. – (minus sign) – indicates subtraction
3. × (multiplication sign) – indicates multiplication
4. ÷ (division sign) – indicates division
5. = (equals sign) – indicates equality
6. ≠ (not equal sign) – indicates inequality
7. < (less than sign) – indicates that one quantity is less than another
8. > (greater than sign) – indicates that one quantity is greater than another
> 
>
9. ≤ (less than or equal to sign) – indicates that one quantity is less than or equal to another
10. ≥ (greater than or equal to sign) – indicates that one quantity is greater than or equal to another
11. ∑ (summation symbol) – indicates the sum of a sequence of numbers or variables
12. ∫ (integral symbol) – indicates integration or finding the area under a curve
13. √ (square root symbol) – indicates finding the square root of a number
14. π (pi symbol) – represents the ratio of the circumference of a circle to its diameter, approximately equal to 3.14159
15. θ (theta symbol) – commonly used to represent an angle in geometry
16. ⊥ (perpendicular symbol) – used to indicate that two lines or segments are perpendicular to each other

These symbols are just a few of the many used in mathematics. Understanding these symbols is essential to communicating mathematical ideas effectively, and will be useful throughout your studies in math.

Protestantism, which is a branch of Christianity, has played a significant role in shaping ideological thinking in the United States. Protestantism has a long and varied history in the United States, and it has shaped the views and actions of individuals and groups on a wide range of issues.

One area where Protestantism has had a significant influence is on social and political issues. Protestantism has a tradition of social activism and has often been at the forefront of movements for social change, such as the abolition of slavery, the civil rights movement, and the fight against poverty and inequality. Protestant denominations have also been influential in shaping the positions of political parties and other groups on a wide range of social and political issues.

Protestantism has also played a role in shaping ideological thinking on issues related to personal responsibility and individualism. Protestantism has a tradition of emphasizing the importance of personal responsibility and individual effort, and these values have been influential in shaping the views and actions of individuals and groups on issues such as education, healthcare, and economic policy.

Overall, while Protestantism is just one of many religious traditions that have shaped ideological thinking in the United States, it has had a significant influence on the country's political and social landscape, particularly on issues related to social justice and personal responsibility.

Thank you for providing the schema. Based on the information given, I will assume that the `public.event` table contains the information about the sounds detected by the devices, and the `public.sound` table contains the categories of sounds.

Now, let's create a query to get the breakdown of sound categories in the top 90% and the remaining 10% as "other".
```sql
WITH sound_counts AS (
    SELECT
        s.id,
        s.name,
        COUNT(e.id) AS event_count
    FROM
        public.sound s
        LEFT JOIN public.event e ON s.id = e.sound_id
    GROUP BY
        s.id,
        s.name
),
total_count AS (
    SELECT
        SUM(event_count) AS total_event_count
    FROM
        sound_counts
),
cumulative_counts AS (
    SELECT
        id,
        name,
        event_count,
        SUM(event_count) OVER (ORDER BY event_count DESC) AS cumulative_event_count,
        total_event_count
    FROM
        sound_counts, total_count
),
percentages AS (
    SELECT
        id,
        name,
        event_count,
        cumulative_event_count,
        total_event_count,
        (cumulative_event_count::float / total_event_count::float) * 100 AS cumulative_percentage
    FROM
        cumulative_counts
)

SELECT
    id,
    name,
    event_count,
    cumulative_event_count,
    total_event_count,
    cumulative_percentage,
    CASE
        WHEN cumulative_percentage <= 90 THEN name
        ELSE 'other'
    END AS category
FROM
    percentages;
```
This query calculates the cumulative percentage of each sound category and groups the categories that contribute to more than 90% of the events as the top categories and the remaining categories as "other".

Ah, I understand now. The tuple you provided describes a context-free grammar, which is a type of formal grammar that is different from a regular grammar.

A context-free grammar consists of four components:

* A set of terminals, which are the basic building blocks of the language, in this case {a, b, c, d}
* A set of non-terminals, which are used to generate the language, in this case {S, A, B, C, D}
* A set of production rules, which describe how the non-terminals can be replaced with terminals and other non-terminals to generate strings in the language, in this case:
	+ S → A
	+ S → B
	+ A → aS
	+ A → cA
	+ B → b
	+ B → bB
	+ B → CD
	+ C → c
	+ C →
	+ D → d
	+ D →
* A start symbol, which is the non-terminal from which the grammar starts generating the language, in this case S

This grammar generates a set of string that can be represented by this set of rules, for example:

* S -> A -> aS -> acS -> accS ->
